CQC Inspections will report on “Duty of Candour” under the Key Question of Safety – if the care provided does not reflect the required characteristics of “Good” (as defined in the CQC Provider Handbook), then inspections are recommended to assess whether the service “Requires Improvement” or “Inadequate”, and whether there has been a breach of the regulation.

As this is an issue that affects patient safety, any information received from a member of the public or Practice staff relating to Duty of Candour will be investigated in line with the CQC’s Safeguarding and Whistleblowing protocols where relevant.

Recognising an Incident

The relevance of the Duty of Candour begins with an acknowledgement that as the result of a safety incident, a patient has suffered moderate or major harm, or has died.

an Incident

The relevance of the Duty of Candour begins with an acknowledgement that as the result of a safety incident, a patient has suffered moderate or major harm, or has died.

As soon as an incident has occurred or been identified;

Clinical care must be administered to prevent further harm.

If any additional treatment is necessary, it should happen as soon as reasonably practicable after discussing with the patient (or carer if the patient is unable to participate in discussion) and with the appropriate consent.

Moderate / severe incidents, or any incidents that result in the death of a patient, must be reported to the patient or next of kin (with the appropriate consent) within a maximum of 10 working days from the incident being reported.

The initial notification of the incident must be verbal (face to face where possible), unless the patient/carer/family cannot be contacted or decline notification.

An explanation and a sincere expression of apology must be provided verbally and recorded. At the time of the incident, an initial apology and explanation must be given.

The Patient/Carer must be offered a written notification of the incident along with a sincere apology.

and recorded. At the time of the incident, an initial apology and explanation must be given.

The Patient/Carer must be offered a written notification of the incident along with a sincere apology.

A step by step explanation of the incident must be offered as soon as it is practicably possible, even if this is an initial view pending investigation of the incident.

The Practice must maintain full written documentation of any letters, discussions, meetings during this investigation, including the response from any of the patients/carers. If any meetings or interviews are offered and declined, then there must be a record of this.

Once the investigation has been completed and a final report has been made, the results should be shared with patient/relatives/carers within 10 working days.

Summary of CQC Regulation 20 : Duty of Candour

When a notifiable safety incident has occurred, the relevant person must be informed as soon as reasonably practicable after the incident has been identified, up to a maximum of 10 working days (as per the NHS Standard Contract).

has occurred, the relevant person must be informed as soon as reasonably practicable after the incident has been identified, up to a maximum of 10 working days (as per the NHS Standard Contract).

All staff must have responsibility to adhere to that organisation’s policies and procedures around duty of candour, regardless of their level of seniority or whether they are permanent, temporary/casual members of staff.

The ‘Being Open Framework’ provides guidance on how to ensure good communication with the patient, their families and carers.

Regulation 20 defines what constitutes a notifiable safety incident. It includes incidents that could result in, or appear to have resulted in, the death of the person using the service or severe harm, moderate harm, or prolonged psychological harm.

Where the degree of harm is not yet clear, the relevant person must be informed of the notifiable safety incident in line with the requirements of the regulation.

The Practice is not required by the regulation to inform a person using the service when a ‘near miss’ has occurred, and the incident has resulted in no harm to that person.

There must be appropriate arrangements place to notify the person using the service who is affected by an incident if they are;

has occurred, and the incident has resulted in no harm to that person.

There must be appropriate arrangements place to notify the person using the service who is affected by an incident if they are;

16 years and over and

lack capacity to make a decision regarding their care or treatment (as determined in accordance with sections 2 and 3 of the 2005 Mental Capacity Act)

this includes ensuring that a person acting lawfully on their behalf (e.g. persons acting as Carer) is notified as the relevant person.

A person acting lawfully on behalf of the person (e.g. persons acting as Carer) using the service must be notified as the relevant person where the person using the service is under 16 and not competent to make a decision regarding their care or treatment.

A person acting lawfully on behalf of the person (e.g. persons acting as Carer) using the service must be notified as the relevant person, upon the death of the person using the service.

Other than the situations outlined above, information should only be disclosed to family members or carers where the person using the service has given their express or implied consent.

the service.

Other than the situations outlined above, information should only be disclosed to family members or carers where the person using the service has given their express or implied consent.

A step-by-step account of all relevant facts known about the incident at the time must be given, in person, by one or more appropriate representatives of the Practice. This should include as much or as little information as the relevant person wants to know, be jargon free and explain any complicated terms.

The account of the facts must be given in a manner that the relevant person can understand. For example, the Practice should consider whether interpreters, advocates, communication aids etc. should be used, while being conscious of any potential breaches of confidentiality in doing so.

The Practice must also explain to the relevant person what further enquiries they will make.

aids etc. should be used, while being conscious of any potential breaches of confidentiality in doing so.

The Practice must also explain to the relevant person what further enquiries they will make.

The Practice must ensure that a meaningful apology is given, in person, by one or more appropriate representatives of the Practice to relevant persons. An apology is defined in the regulation as an expression of sorrow or regret. The NHS Litigation Authority has produced guidance on making an apology (http://www.nhsla.com/claims/Documents/Saying%20Sorry%20-%20Leaflet.pdf), which states that saying sorry is not an admission of legal liability.

In making a decision about who is most appropriate to provide the notification and/or apology, the Practice should consider seniority, relationship to the person using the service, and experience and expertise in the type of notifiable incident that has occurred. The Being Open Framework referenced below provides guidance on this.

The Practice must give the relevant person all reasonable support necessary to help overcome the physical, psychological and emotional impact of the incident.

This could include all or some of the following:

Treating them with respect, consideration and empathy.

necessary to help overcome the physical, psychological and emotional impact of the incident.

This could include all or some of the following:

Treating them with respect, consideration and empathy.

Offering the option of direct emotional support during the notifications, for example from a family member, a friend, a care professional or a trained advocate.

Offering access to assistance with understanding what is being said e.g. via interpretative services, non-verbal communication aids, written information, Braille etc.

Providing access to any necessary treatment and care to recover from or minimise the harm caused where appropriate.

Providing the relevant person with details of specialist independent sources of practical advice and support or emotional support/counselling.

Providing the relevant person with information about available impartial advocacy and support services, their local Health watch and other relevant support groups, for example Cruse Bereavement Care and Action against Medical Accidents (AvMA), to help them deal with the outcome of the incident.

Arranging for care and treatment to be delivered by another professional, team or provider if this is possible, should the relevant person wish.

to help them deal with the outcome of the incident.

Arranging for care and treatment to be delivered by another professional, team or provider if this is possible, should the relevant person wish.

Providing support to access its complaints procedure.

The Being Open Framework referenced below provides guidance on how to support patients, their families and carers when a patient safety incident has occurred.

The Practice must ensure that written notification is given to the relevant person following the notification that was given in person, even though enquiries may not yet be complete.

The written notification must contain all the information that was provided in person including an apology, as well as the results of any enquiries that have been made since the notification in person.

The outcomes or results of any further enquiries and investigations must also be provided in writing to the relevant person through further written notifications, should they wish to receive them.

The Practice must make every reasonable attempt to contact the relevant person through all available communication means. All attempts to contact the relevant person must be documented.

them.

The Practice must make every reasonable attempt to contact the relevant person through all available communication means. All attempts to contact the relevant person must be documented.

If the relevant person does not wish to communicate with the Practice, their wishes must be respected and a record of this must be kept.

If the relevant person has died and there is nobody who can lawfully act on their behalf, a record of this should be kept.

text:
Employee Personal and Environmental Risk Assessment Form

It is the joint responsibility of line managers and staff to complete a risk assessment for those staff at higher risk of COVID-19 infection or possibly disproportionately affected by COVID-19, as set out by Public Health England (PHE) and NHS England. As not all underlying health conditions are routinely declared, staff are encouraged to speak to their managers if they feel they need a risk assessment linked to a previously undeclared issue to enable appropriate support to be provided.

The aim of this assessment is to understand the specific risks staff members may face related to exposure to COVID-19 and enable these risks to be mitigated as far as possible.

All staff are expected to undertake their role and where they are patient facing deal with patients based on their clinical needs, in accordance with their personal and professional responsibility, unless precluded based on their individual risk assessment.

Based on national guidance we are undertaking social distancing measures in order to reduce the transmission of coronavirus (COVID-19) between individuals.

Social Distancing measures:

Based on national guidance we are undertaking social distancing measures in order to reduce the transmission of coronavirus (COVID-19) between individuals.

Social Distancing measures:

Avoid contact with someone who is displaying symptoms of coronavirus (COVID-19). These symptoms include high temperature and/or new and continuous cough, a loss or change to sense of smell or taste;

Avoid non-essential use of public transport, varying your travel times to avoid rush hour, when possible; Work from home, where possible.

Avoid large gatherings, and gatherings in smaller public spaces such as pubs, cinemas, restaurants, theatres, bars, clubs.

Avoid gatherings with friends and family. Keep in touch using remote technology such as phone, internet, and social media.

Use telephone or online services to contact your GP or other essential services.

It is important that all sections of this form are completed in order to provide a comprehensive assessment of risk and agree actions and mitigations for the protection of the staff member, colleagues and patients. This form must be filled in electronically.

pregnancy risk assessment)

Is the role patient facing? Can it be adjusted to reduce patient exposure?

If the employee remains patient facing, avoid, where possible, caring for patients with suspected or confirmed coronavirus infection.  If this is not possible, use PPE and ensure a thorough risk assessment is undertaken.

F2 ‘How to’ Guide: Riverside Practice

Contents

Introduction	1

Sending a task	1

Order blood tests	2

Order imaging	3

Order ECG/ echo / 24h tape	4

2 week wait referrals	5

Advice and guidance	6

Referral letters	8

A&E referrals	8

Telephone advice from Homerton specialties	8

Introduction

Welcome to the Riverside Practice! I did my rotation here from December – April 2025. I put together this guide to help with the most common admin tasks that initially take a while to get to grips with. I hope you find it helpful and it makes the transition from Cerner to Emis a little easier. Any feedback or suggestions for updates or new sections would be much appreciated. Please email Kieran.mclaverty1@nhs.net. Enjoy your time at the practice.

Sending a task

You will often be sending tasks to reception to send off referrals and other admin issues. Select the purple task icon…

Once clicked, make sure the ‘type’ is patient note, and ‘send to’ reception. Remember to check the urgent box at the bottom for important tasks like 2ww referrals

If you have an urgent message then send a note instead (yellow icon), and it will pop up on their  screen as an instant notification. This is good if you need to ask Dr Goel or Dr Rishi if you can call them to discuss a case

then send a note instead (yellow icon), and it will pop up on their  screen as an instant notification. This is good if you need to ask Dr Goel or Dr Rishi if you can call them to discuss a case

Order blood tests

Once you have opened a consultation, select ‘test requests’ and ‘online’ requests…

Select Homerton University Hospital on the Next page

Then on the request form always select Dr Goel

Go to the ‘order’ tab and this is where you can select bloods. You can also select wound swabs / FIT tests/ urine MSU etc. from here.  Most of these can be selected from the left panel. If you need to find more niche bloods you can search by clicking open:

Add clinical details and then hit save at the bottom

You can then tell the patient to pick up a blood test form from reception. They book the test themselves at a local clinic. If they are frail reception can book them in to be done here.

Order imaging

Imaging uses the same process as bloods (see above). You can either scroll down the panel or search for it like you would a blood test

XR imaging is done via walk-in. Book the scan and tell patient to go to Homerton 9am-5pm Monday to Friday. Tell them to pick up a form from reception for this (but this is not vital)

a blood test

XR imaging is done via walk-in. Book the scan and tell patient to go to Homerton 9am-5pm Monday to Friday. Tell them to pick up a form from reception for this (but this is not vital)

Ultrasound is done by appointment only. Once you book the ultrasound the hospital will contact the patient with the appointment date. Same with CT and MRI.  You don’t need to do anything more. Importantly, if the ultrasound is urgent e.g. new neck mass – select this in the pull down tab and write in the description!

If you want to image a mass or skin lump – the best option to select for is ‘US SOFT TISSUE’

Order ECG/ echo / 24h tape

These are all referral forms that you need to fill out and task reception to send off. Once you have your consultation open select document 🡪 create letter…

Hit the magnifying icon to open up the full list of referral forms…

Then to get to the referral forms click on the below files…

Fill in the form and task reception with sending it off to the Homerton.

2 week wait referrals

These are also found under documents, click on ‘pan London urgent suspected cancer referrals’ and choose from the list

and task reception with sending it off to the Homerton.

2 week wait referrals

These are also found under documents, click on ‘pan London urgent suspected cancer referrals’ and choose from the list

Once you have completed the form, don’t forget to send an ‘urgent’ task reception to send this off,  otherwise the referral won’t be made.

As a safety net you should set a reminder to check up on the patient in 2 weeks to see if they have had their appointment. These reminders will go to Dr Goel.  Hit F12 (Iqra can set up this shortcut for you) then launch CEG templates…

Then ‘cancer referrals and safety netting’…

Advice and guidance

This is a service where you can ask for specialist advice. They tend to reply quickly – within one week. If the specialist feels wants to see the patient in clinic they can convert the advice to a referral. Add the following entry in EMIS for audit “consultant advice service used”.

Select referrals on the top bar then the e-RS tab…

to see the patient in clinic they can convert the advice to a referral. Add the following entry in EMIS for audit “consultant advice service used”.

Select referrals on the top bar then the e-RS tab…

Most of the time you will be asking for ‘advice’, and ‘routine’ priority. You do not need to put any clinical term in. Find the right specialty. Choose ‘not otherwise specified’ for the clinic type otherwise you will get only specialist centres at other hospitals. The challenge is making sure the Homerton comes up in the list of available hospitals.

Then choose advice and guidance at the Homerton…

And select request in the bottom right corner

To check on your A&G replies, navigate to the eRs homepage and select ‘worklists’

Referral letters

choose advice and guidance at the Homerton…

And select request in the bottom right corner

To check on your A&G replies, navigate to the eRs homepage and select ‘worklists’

Referral letters

For routine outpatient referrals first discuss if appropriate with Dr Goel or Dr Rishi. If agreed add the following code in EMIS, “peer review of prospective referral carried out” The process for routine referrals can be a little complicated. For most medical specialties you can write a generic letter and task reception with sending this. This generic letter can be found in the same documents tab that you use for 2ww referrals / ECGs etc. Search for ‘Dr Goel Standard Template’ and fill in the letter. Make sure to address the right department at the top .

Some referrals require you to fill in a specialised form. If in doubt ask Dr Goel / Dr Rishi about this. The most common ones that do this are: rapid access chest pain, respiratory diagnostic hublets (for spirometry)  community physiotherapy, ACERS, Mental health referral forms, teledermatology (asking for advice on pictures of skin problems) and foot clinic.

A&E referrals

diagnostic hublets (for spirometry)  community physiotherapy, ACERS, Mental health referral forms, teledermatology (asking for advice on pictures of skin problems) and foot clinic.

A&E referrals

Will require discussion with Dr Goel or Dr Rishi first. Send a patient with a printed referral letter – you can modify a ‘Dr Goel Standard Template’ and include the details here. You could also send this letter to their phone / email via AccuRx for them to show up at the Homerton.

If you are sending them for a surgical or paediatric review, then call ahead via Homerton switch to inform them.

Add the following in EMIS for audit, “referral to accident and emergency service”

Telephone advice from Homerton specialties

An extensive list of all the urgent advice contact numbers can be found here: Urgent and Emergency Care - GP Website

The most frequently used advice lines I use are;

Paediatric consultant: 07795390715: this is operational 9-5 Mon-Fri. To be used for non-urgent advice to discuss any complex cases. E.g. should this patient be referred to clinic? Any more tests we could do in GP? If you think the child should go to A&E then discuss with paeds SpR via switch

advice to discuss any complex cases. E.g. should this patient be referred to clinic? Any more tests we could do in GP? If you think the child should go to A&E then discuss with paeds SpR via switch

SDEC: 07500991259 open from 10-6pm, run by A&E or Acute Medicine consultant. Helpful  if a patient needs urgent investigations / bloods but does likely not need admission into hospital. E.g. cough, borderline obs, but otherwise well – to arrange CXR and bloods.

Mental health 02038194957 (crisis mental health line). To discuss any acute concerns over MH. Often these teams will arrange follow up in the community. If they need urgent review they can be sent to the Raybould Crisis Hub, which is a walk-in centre next to HUH.

Data Protection Update: 04/2020				Number 10

Freedom of Information Requests

Background

Freedom of Information (FOI) requests arise from the Freedom of Information Act 2000. These requests are different from Subject Access Requests that arise from the General Data Protection Regulation.

It is probable that practices may experience rising numbers of these and need to work out a protocol as a result. The good news is that you can treat these in a similar fashion to a Subject Access Request and log them in the same way. The bad news is that some of the requirements are different.

Under the FOI ,the definition of a public authority includes GP Practices but the only requests that require a response from you are those that cover the work you do for the NHS. Requests relating to private work the practice undertakes as a business are NOT subject to FOI requests and can be refused.

What is a Freedom of Information request?

Any member of the public can make a request for information of an authority receiving public funding.

A public authority is under a duty to inform the person making the request:

Whether it holds the information as set out in the request (this applies to information in both electronic and paper forms) and if it does;

is under a duty to inform the person making the request:

Whether it holds the information as set out in the request (this applies to information in both electronic and paper forms) and if it does;

To communicate the information requested.

You are not compelled to create NEW information: for instance to undertake a requested new analysis of your data. However, if the analysis has already been done and exists then you must release it.

Information you normally give out anyway, such as on your policies and procedures, does not come under the Act. Requests concerning environmental information are covered by the Environmental Information Regulations 2004 but should be treated along the lines of the guidance issued here, except that you have only 20 working days to respond. Personal information concerning the person making the request comes under a Subject Access Request.

Dealing with the request

A valid request under the FOI must be in writing not verbally (unlike the GDPR) and clearly state the name and address (either postal or e-mail) and will clearly outline what is being asked for. If the request is unclear you must ask the requester for clarification if, no response is then received after 2 months, you can mark the request as closed.

clearly outline what is being asked for. If the request is unclear you must ask the requester for clarification if, no response is then received after 2 months, you can mark the request as closed.

If the request meets these tests then the obligation upon the practice is to search for the information “in a reasonable and intelligent way based upon an understanding of how the (practice) manages its records”. If a search conducted in this way does not reveal the information, then you may consider that, “on the balance of probabilities”, the practice does not hold it. If you believe that a different authority holds the information sought then you should say so and provide the contact details.

However, the written request from a member of the public does not need to mention the FOI Act so in practice it is necessary to treat any written request as though it was made under the Act

Normally, you have 20 working days in which to complete the request. The count begins on the day you receive the request.

Please be aware that under section 77 of the FOI Act it is a criminal offence to “alter, block, erase, destroy or conceal” any information subject to the request.

on the day you receive the request.

Please be aware that under section 77 of the FOI Act it is a criminal offence to “alter, block, erase, destroy or conceal” any information subject to the request.

Interestingly please be aware that if the Practice maintains a public social media presence such as a Facebook account, a valid FOI request can be made via Facebook and responded to in the same way.

Advice and Assistance

Section 16 of the Act places a duty upon public authorities to provide “reasonable advice and assistance” to applicants requesting information. This duty is enforceable by the Information Commissioner’s Office. What does this mean?

You should have a postal information and e-mail address where requests can be sent. (In practice add this to your website and noticeboard)

If an individual, because of disability or language, cannot make a request in writing the practice should take a view whether they are prepared to accept a verbal request or to signpost the individual to Citizens’ Advice to help them frame their written request.

Ability to Charge

Unlike under the GDPR, you are able to charge for providing the information to an FOI request although many public authorities do not.

to help them frame their written request.

Ability to Charge

Unlike under the GDPR, you are able to charge for providing the information to an FOI request although many public authorities do not.

You can only charge for the production and release of the information itself and not for the act of searching for it. If you decide to charge then you must notify the requester as soon as possible within the 20 day period. It is reasonable to set a period of 3 months within which the fee is to be paid. The requester has the right to complain about the fee via your internal complaints procedure and to the Information Commissioner.

You may charge for:

The actual costs of producing the information, e.g redaction of personal information as well as printing and photocopying;

Transmission costs, e.g. postage, cost of memory stick etc.

Other costs associated with the applicant’s preferred method of receiving the information.

Please note that the ability to charge can be dictated by the terms of your GMS/PMS contract. Please check if in doubt.

associated with the applicant’s preferred method of receiving the information.

Please note that the ability to charge can be dictated by the terms of your GMS/PMS contract. Please check if in doubt.

There is an appropriate cost limit of course. The limit is calculated at £25 per hour for 18 hours or £450. If your estimate is that the costs of provision will be above this limit you can refuse to deal with the request. When assessing whether this limit will be breached, you will need to assess the effort required to locate and retrieve the information and extract the data from other documents and systems. This should be reached by means of a “reasonable assessment” and not a precise calculation.

One strange disconnection is that while you are under a GDPR derived duty not to reveal the data of specific third parties in an FOI request, you cannot include the time taken for such a reduction in your assessment; however if the “burden (of multiple redactions) is unreasonable “ you can refuse the request altogether under section 14 below.

cannot include the time taken for such a reduction in your assessment; however if the “burden (of multiple redactions) is unreasonable “ you can refuse the request altogether under section 14 below.

If it is assessed that more than 18 hours work will be required, the request can be refused. (Section 12 of the Act) but the refusal may also include suggesting how the scope of the request could be restricted in order to come within scope.

The details of every request you refuse as vexatious, together with the dates of request received and notice given should be included as part of your FOI/SAR log.

Hard to Resolve Requests

Some requests touch on information that relates to other, specified people (data subjects under the GDPR) or may even affect their interests. Although the boundaries are not clear, it is probable that once a different, specific individual is named, the GDPR will take precedence. If in doubt, consult your Data Protection Officer.

Occasionally, the request may include contract or other information that is the preserve of other organisations. It is wise to consult these third parties and ask them whether you can release that data or whether they would like to take over the response.

information that is the preserve of other organisations. It is wise to consult these third parties and ask them whether you can release that data or whether they would like to take over the response.

Any request asking “Why?” does not fall within the parameters of the FOI and does not require an answer if it would mean creating new data, unless you wish to provide an explanation.

If a request requires extensive information or you need to consult other organisations or individuals you will qualify for a “public interest test extension until such time as is reasonable in the circumstances”. Best practice says that this amounts to a further 20 working days and that you should inform the requester.

Disputes

Complaints about the handling of FOI requests should be subject to the practice’s normal complaints process, but a dispute unresolved by this process is likely to lead to a full internal review of the decisions around the information released or non-release of information. Such an internal review should be carried out by someone unconnected with the original decisions. Contact your DPO if you cannot do this otherwise internally. The requester must also be informed of their right to complain to the Information Commissioner if they are dissatisfied.

decisions. Contact your DPO if you cannot do this otherwise internally. The requester must also be informed of their right to complain to the Information Commissioner if they are dissatisfied.

Vexatious Requests

As so often, the definition of what exactly is a vexatious request which can be refused is very vague. Guidance from the ICO gives some indicators:

An abusive or offensive request is clearly vexatious

The burden it places upon the organisation is unreasonable

Questionable motives lie behind the request

The potential value of the information requested – something like how many tea-bags do the nurses use would be an example or worthless information

Harassing the organisation with continual requests.

Has the requester made a number of other FOI requests.

Previous dealings with the requester (i.e is there history of a difficult relationship that could indicate a grudge or desire to cause trouble for the organisation.

If any of these conditions apply, the request can be refused under Section 14 of the Act, but organisations should always seek to refuse under section 12 first. Repeated vexatious requests do not require more than the original refusal notice to be issued.

under Section 14 of the Act, but organisations should always seek to refuse under section 12 first. Repeated vexatious requests do not require more than the original refusal notice to be issued.

The details of every request you regard as vexatious and why, together with the dates of request received and notice given should be included as part of your FOI/SAR log.

Publication Schemes

Section 19 of the FOI Act requires all public authorities to adopt and maintain a publication scheme. An example scheme is attached at Annex A for you to use on your website and in paper format.

FOI Compliance Statistics

Public authorities with over 100 (FTE) employees are encouraged to publish statistics on their performance in handling FOI requests. This is unlikely to apply to most GP surgeries, but you will need to keep a log for inspection purposes.

GP Pay & Benefits

It is a contractual requirement to publish these on your website annually already.

Hospitality and register of gifts

You should already be keeping a separate register of these.

Third party Contracts

is a contractual requirement to publish these on your website annually already.

Hospitality and register of gifts

You should already be keeping a separate register of these.

Third party Contracts

If the practice sub-contracts to a third party a service for patients, as a public authority the practice remains responsible for answering FOI requests, but you should specify in the agreement that the contractor is responsible for providing information in response to FOI requests regarding that service. Contractors receiving FOI requests should pass them on to the practice to formulate the response. There is a joint responsibility to inform the public:

how the contractor can be approached;

the time limits that apply;

how any disagreement concerning the provision of information in response to a FOI request can be addressed with contractor;

how a request for an internal review by the Practice can be handled

the contractor’ responsibility for maintaining adequate systems for record keeping in relation to the service.

These considerations should be contained within the contract or as part of a separate Memorandum of Understanding.

for maintaining adequate systems for record keeping in relation to the service.

These considerations should be contained within the contract or as part of a separate Memorandum of Understanding.

Confidentiality clauses can conflict with your obligations under the Act and your broader transparency obligations to make available details of spending, tenders and contracts. Under common law a breach of a duty of (commercial) confidentiality is not enforceable in the courts where an overriding public interests justifies the breach.

Questions regarding the management of the service are likely to remain the responsibility of the practice but the contractor’s agreement to disclosure should be sought.

ANNEX 1

How to make a Freedom of Information request to           The Riverside Practice

The Freedom of Information Act 2000 provides a right of access to a wide range of information held by public authorities, including the NHS and GP practices.  The purpose is to promote greater openness and accountability about what we do.

Our duty to you

The Freedom of Information (FOI) Act 2000 requires us to:

Provide information to you about our practice through a publication scheme

Provide a guide to this information

Respond appropriately to requests for information

(FOI) Act 2000 requires us to:

Provide information to you about our practice through a publication scheme

Provide a guide to this information

Respond appropriately to requests for information

Before you request information from us.

Check whether the information you seek is already available on our website. NHS England publishes information on its website also and you may well find the answer to your question is already there.

Our FOI publication scheme below sets out what information we currently have available or expect to release.

If you request information from us that is published elsewhere, we will refer you to the published source.

Who can request information?

Anyone, anywhere in the world, can make a FOI request of this practice

What can I request?

You can seek any recorded information that you think the Riverside Practice may hold.

Freedom of Information publication scheme

This section explains what information is routinely available and how it can be accessed.

information that you think the Riverside Practice may hold.

Freedom of Information publication scheme

This section explains what information is routinely available and how it can be accessed.

The Publication Scheme is designed to signpost individuals to information we proactively release as and when it becomes available. The Publication Scheme contains seven classes of information, as follows, and information falling into each of these classes is published on our website:

Who we are and what we do

Financial information including GP earnings

What are our service priorities and information as to how we are doing

How decisions are made in conjunction with our practice policy

Our policies and procedures

Statistical data including patient list size

The services we offer

At present, all the information we release proactively is available free of charge on our website. If in the future, we make information available but do not publish it on our website we will update this publication scheme to explain how you can access it. We will also explain any charges that may be necessary.

we make information available but do not publish it on our website we will update this publication scheme to explain how you can access it. We will also explain any charges that may be necessary.

These charges will be justified and transparent and kept to a minimum. Charges may be made for information subject to a charging regime specified by Parliament. Charges may be made for actual disbursements incurred such as:

photocopying

postage and packaging

the costs directly incurred as a result of locating viewing and collating information

Charges may also be made for information provided under this scheme where they are legally authorised, they are in all the circumstances, including the general principles of the right of access to information held by public authorities, justified and are in accordance with a published schedule or schedules of fees which is readily available to the public.

principles of the right of access to information held by public authorities, justified and are in accordance with a published schedule or schedules of fees which is readily available to the public.

Charges may also be made for making datasets (or parts of datasets) that are relevant copyright works available for re-use. These charges will be in accordance with either regulations made under section 11B of the Freedom of Information Act or other enactments. Information held by a public authority that is not published under this scheme can be requested in writing, when its provision will be considered in accordance with the provisions of the Freedom of Information Act

If the information is environmental, we will respond according to the Environmental Information Regulations (EIR)  2004. You do not have to know whether the information you want is covered by the EIR or the FOI Act. When you make a request, we will decide which law applies.

If the information relates to your own personal data,  then you should make a subject access request under the Data Protection Act  (DPA) 2018, and not under the FOI Act. See below for how to make DPA requests.

How do I request information?

Your request must be in writing and can be either posted or emailed to

Protection Act  (DPA) 2018, and not under the FOI Act. See below for how to make DPA requests.

How do I request information?

Your request must be in writing and can be either posted or emailed to

For postal requests, please send to the following address: The Riverside Practice,   14 Urban Hive, Theydon Road London E5 9BQ.

Email requests should be sent to nelondonicb.theriversidepractice@nhs.net

Please write “Freedom of Information” in the subject line.

If you would have difficulty making a written request, please call us on                   020 8806 1928.

What information must I include in my request?

The FOI Act requires certain information to be supplied before we can respond to your request:

your real name – we do not have to respond to requests submitted under a pseudonym;

your address (email addresses are acceptable);

a description of the information you wish to obtain; and

any preferences for the format in which you wish to receive the information e.g. electronic or hard copy. We will endeavour to meet your preferences but cannot guarantee that we will be able to.

What you do not need to do:

explicitly mention the FOI Act, although it will help to do so;

aim to complete internal reviews within 20 working days, although more cases that are complex may take longer. Where internal reviews go over 20 working days, we will keep you informed of progress.

If, after an internal review, you are still not satisfied you can then complain to the Information Commissioner (ICO).  Details of how to do this are available at the ICO website.

Details of how to complain further to the Information Commissioner will be included in our response to your internal review request.

How do I request information held about me?

This falls under the General Data Protection Regulation as incorporated in the Data Protection Act 2018. Please refer to separate guidance on the Practice website or contact reception.

text:
The Riverside Practice - General Practice Privacy Notice (Including special provisions under the Covid 19 pandemic).

Background

This practice’s primary purpose is to provide the best care possible for you. In order to do this, we need to collect, store and share information about you.

This privacy notice is designed to explain what happens to any personal data that you give us or any information concerning you that is collected by other organisations, for instance, if you attend an Accident and Emergency department. This includes how your data is held and/or processed by us.

This notice includes:

Who we are and how we use your information

The kinds of information we hold and how we process them

The legal grounds for processing your personal data, including when it is shared with others

What to do if your personal information changes

The length of time that your information is stored and retained by us

Information about your rights under the 2018 Data Protection Act incorporating the UK General Data Protection Regulations (GDPR)

Information about what to do if you have a query or problem

text:
by us

Information about your rights under the 2018 Data Protection Act incorporating the UK General Data Protection Regulations (GDPR)

Information about what to do if you have a query or problem

Under the 2018 Data Protection Act incorporating the UK General Data Protection Regulation –(GDPR) the practice is known as the Data Controller. As such, we are responsible for keeping your data up to date and accurate, as well as storing it safely and sharing it securely. If you have a problem or a question regarding your data, you should contact the Practice Manager in the first instance. Should your ussue be unresolved, the Act stipulates also that public sector organisations should provide access to an independent Data Protection Officer and their contact details are provided in the summary below.

The information we hold on you

docTitle: General Practice Privacy Notice Direct Care v12 April 22 (002)(1)

text:
also that public sector organisations should provide access to an independent Data Protection Officer and their contact details are provided in the summary below.

The information we hold on you

Our practice keeps data on you relating to who you are, where you live, your contact details, your family, details of your occupation -if any – and, possibly, your employers, your lifestyle, your health problems and diagnoses, the reasons you seek help, as well, at your appointments. Your record also contains details if you have a carer, where you are seen, when you are seen, and who by: as well as all referrals to specialists and other health and social care providers, tests carried out here and in other places, investigations and scans, treatments and outcomes of treatments, your treatment history, the observations and opinions of other health care workers, within and without the NHS as well as comments and aide memoires reasonably made by health and care professionals in this practice who are appropriately involved in your health care. All of this data helps us in providing you with the best possible care, and, as quickly as possible in an emergency.

text:
Provisions during the Covid 19 pandemic

The NHS faces continued severe pressure during the pandemic. This makes it even more important to share health and care data across relevant organisations.

Using Regulation 3 (4) of the Health Service (Control of Patient Information) Regulations 2002 and related legislation, the Secretary of State for Health has issued a notice (the COPI notice) that requires health organisations including GP surgeries, local authorities and government bodies to share confidential patient information for the duration of the pandemic. There are new services and information flows that have been set up to manage the outbreak. For instance, this practice is part of a Primary Care Network and a GP Federation. As such, it collaborates to deliver COVID vaccinations and treatments in this area and is part of a ‘buddy system’ so that if its staff are so affected by the virus, that the practice cannot operate, colleagues from other practices and other organisations can still provide you with care.

docTitle: General Practice Privacy Notice Direct Care v12 April 22 (002)(1)

source.path: rag/sources/General Practice Privacy Notice Direct Care v12 April 22 (002)(1).docx

text:
part of a ‘buddy system’ so that if its staff are so affected by the virus, that the practice cannot operate, colleagues from other practices and other organisations can still provide you with care.

All patients registered with a GP have a Summary Care Record (SCR) unless they have chosen not to have one. This record gives professionals in the healthcare system away from your practice access to your key information when you need it. If you have previously expressed a preference to only have core information shared in the Summary Care Record or to opt out of the SCR completely, these preferences will be respected during the period of the COPI notice. For all other patients, the SCR will be used to share additional information as required. New opt-out requests and changes to your opt-out preferences will be suspended and not processed for the duration of the pandemic.

Automated processing of data will be used to identify vulnerable patients and patients needing to be shielded.

NHS England and NHS Improvement and NHSX have developed a single, secure data-store to gather data from across the health and care system to manage and inform the Covid 19 response.

NHS England and NHS Improvement and NHSX have developed a single, secure data-store to gather data from across the health and care system to manage and inform the Covid 19 response.

Any data-flows used to share data specifically to manage Covid 19 during the pandemic will cease once the COPI notice is withdrawn, unless a full Data Protection Impact Assessment is completed.

Because of the importance of sharing data for us all (defined as “public interest” under the 2018 Data Protection Act) any patient opt-out including the National Data Opt-out will not apply during the COPI notice period. It may also take the practice longer to respond to Data Subject Access Requests (DSARs) than the stipulated one calendar month - the Information Commissioner’s Office has recognised the pressure the pandemic has placed upon GP surgeries.

Who do we share information with

Who do we share information with

As GPs, we cannot provide all your treatment ourselves, so we need to delegate this responsibility to others within the practice and with other organisations such as pharmacies or hospitals. This practice also pools resources with other primary care organisations in the area, including providing extended access services out of normal practice hours, or to provide other specialist clinics. Such services may be provided as part of our local Primary Care Network (PCN) or in conjunction with other PCNs.

As such, if your care requires treatment outside the practice, we will exchange with those providing such care and treatment whatever information may be necessary to provide you with safe, high quality care. The practice also delivers services and treatment to our patients as part of, and in association with local primary care networks and beyond.

Once you have seen any outside care provider, they will normally send us details of the care they have provided you with, so that we can understand and support your health and treatment better and update your health care record.

However, we will gladly discuss this with you in more detail if you would like to know more. We keep a register of our Information Assets which also sets out a Record of Processing Activity. The majority of patient data processing and storage happens via our EMIS and EMIS Community patient record systems.

Safeguarding and the Caldicott Guardian

The practice is dedicated to safeguarding all its patients, including children and vulnerable adults. This means that information will be shared by the practice in their best interests. Such decisions are the ultimate responsibility of the practice’s Caldicott Guardian. The Caldicott Guardian is the senior person - always a doctor and often a partner within a practice- responsible for protecting the confidentiality of people’s health and care information. The duty to share data for the benefit of individuals can be more important as the duty to protect patient confidentiality, and actions taken as a result of safeguarding concerns will override data protection. The decision of the Caldicott Guardian is final and there is no appeal process.

Medical Audits and Medicines Management

Medical Audits and Medicines Management

The practice will conduct audits of its services and treatment as well as reviews of medicines prescribed to its patients. Reviews of patient data are necessary to allow us to monitor, test and update our services and prescribing to ensure that you receive the most appropriate and cost-effective treatments. These reviews may take the form of internal audits or those conducted by other commissioned healthcare organisations such as the local Medicine Management Team.

Automated Data Processing and Risk Stratification

Electronic tools of prediction, based upon algorithms and artificial intelligence are used within the NHS to determine a patient’s future risks and treatment needs. Wherever we can, we want to prevent admissions to A&E and secondary care which would be otherwise necessary. Such preventative care may, for instance, use these tools to determine the risk and consequence of a future fall in an elderly patient. Under Covid 19 these tools are being used to identify vulnerable patients and patients who need to be shielded.

Research and Planning

The practice takes part in research that uses anonymised or pseudonymised data. This means that patient data cannot be traced back to individuals and is therefore no longer personal data under the 2018 Data Protection Act.

Anonymised or pseudonymised patient data held by the practice may also be used to evaluate present services that provide direct care or to plan future ones within the practice or across the local area.

Identifiable patient data can be used in planning and managing the response of the NHS, nationally, to the Covid 19 virus. This will continue until the COPI notice above is withdrawn.

Data Opt-Outs (The National Data Opt-out) and Your Right to Object.

You cannot opt-out of your data being shared for the purposes of providing you with direct care. You can exercise your right to object to a specific process involving your data. If you wish to do this for data processed at this practice then you must contact the practice’s Data Protection Officer at chpatients@jem-gdpr.co.uk.

Situations where data is needed in the “public interest”, e.g in cases of epidemic where communicable diseases need to be diagnosed and the spread of their infection prevented or controlled;

To monitor and deliver vaccination programmes

To manage risks of infection from food or water supplies or the environment.

Guidance on Individual’s Rights under GDPR

Background

The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) provides individuals with the following rights:

The right to be informed

The right of access

The right to rectification

The right to erasure

The right to restrict processing

The right to data portability

The right to object

Rights in relation to automated decision making and profiling.

General rules when dealing with individual’s rights

When an individual makes a request they can do so:

Verbally, in writing (hardcopy or digital) and even social media

To any member of staff

as requests can be made to anyone, not just a specific member of staff or contact point, it is vital that all staff who might be asked are trained on what to do if someone requests their data from them

You should have a clear procedure and keep a record of all requests and their outcomes.

You must respond to requests without delay and at most, within one month of the request being made.

Sometimes you might need to confirm someone’s identity to respond to a request. You must ask them for ID as proof as soon as possible. Your time to respond to the request starts as soon as they provide proof of their identity.

confirm someone’s identity to respond to a request. You must ask them for ID as proof as soon as possible. Your time to respond to the request starts as soon as they provide proof of their identity.

Normally you can’t charge people a fee when they make a reasonable request.

You can charge a reasonable fee based on your admin costs if someone makes a request which is ‘manifestly unfounded or excessive’ or if you need to make further copies of someone’s data

If someone makes a request which is ‘manifestly unfounded or excessive’ you can refuse to comply with the request instead of asking for a fee.

If you refuse a request, need to charge a fee or require further information you need to tell the individual as soon as possible and at least within one month:

Why you are refusing the request

That they can complain to the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO) and

That they can see to enforce their rights through a “judicial remedy” i.e through the courts.

The Right to be informed

All individuals have the right to be told about how their personal data is collected and used. You need to provide the people who use your services, your staff, and anyone else whose data you collect with information about:

What information you hold

Why you have it

is collected and used. You need to provide the people who use your services, your staff, and anyone else whose data you collect with information about:

What information you hold

Why you have it

How long you keep it

Who you share it with

You need to provide them with this information in clear and easy to understand language i.e online, in leaflets, posters etc.

You need to provide people with this information when you collect their personal data for example when they are registering with the practice. You need to tell them why you need their information when they are completing their registration forms.

If you receive information about the individual from someone else, you need to provide that individual with privacy information within a reasonable period and at least within one month.

The right of access

People have the right to access their own personal data. Note that this right does not allow other people to request and individual’s data, even if they are related to the individual, unless they have lasting power of attorney or some other written authority to make the request. i.,e. they are a lawyer acting on their behalf.

data, even if they are related to the individual, unless they have lasting power of attorney or some other written authority to make the request. i.,e. they are a lawyer acting on their behalf.

When people ask to have access to their own information, this is called Subject Access Request or SAR. (There is a procedure for SARs in the Record Keeping Policy).

When people make a SAR, they are entitled to:

Confirmation that you have their data

A copy of their data

The information which you would provide in a privacy notice

The right to rectification

Individuals have the right to have their information corrected if it is inaccurate or incomplete.

If someone makes a request to have their information rectified you should:

Record the request

Assess the accuracy of the original information and if it needs to be corrected or completed;

If applicable, correct the mistake. You should keep a clear audit trail of corrected mistakes.

Regardless of outcome, you must let the individual know what decision you have reached.

The right to erasure

This is also called the ‘Right to be Forgotten’

This right applies when:

You use consent for your lawful basis for holding data and the individual withdraws their consent,

right to erasure

This is also called the ‘Right to be Forgotten’

This right applies when:

You use consent for your lawful basis for holding data and the individual withdraws their consent,

You no longer need the data for the purpose you collected it for;

You use legitimate interests as your lawful basis for holding data, the individual objects, and there is no overriding legitimate interest to continue this processing.

You are processing the personal data for direct marketing purposes and the individual objects;

You have processed the personal data unlawfully; or

You have to do it to comply with a legal obligation

If you have disclosed their data to others, or the data has been made public, you must make reasonable steps to tell the other people using the data to erase it.

There are certain instances when the right to erasure does not apply. For Health and Social care providers the main ones are;

When processing is necessary to comply with a legal obligation

When processing is necessary for public health purposes in the public interest

When processing is necessary for the provision of health or social care (if you need their data in order to be able to provide care, you do not need to erase it)

purposes in the public interest

When processing is necessary for the provision of health or social care (if you need their data in order to be able to provide care, you do not need to erase it)

When you comply with an individual’s request to have their data erased you must think of anything you have such as back-ups or archives to make sure that this is also destroyed.

The right to restrict processing

When you are asked to ‘restrict processing’ this means that you can still store the data but you can’t do anything else with it.

The right to restrict processing will often happen in conjunction with the other rights for example:

If someone is concerned that data you have about them is inaccurate, they might asks also that you restrict processing while you investigate their request.

If someone objects to you processing their data, they might ask that you restrict processing while you investigate their request.

You might also have to restrict processing when:

The data has been unlawfully processed but the individual does not want you to erase their data;

processing while you investigate their request.

You might also have to restrict processing when:

The data has been unlawfully processed but the individual does not want you to erase their data;

You don’t need the data anymore but the individual needs it to “establish, exercise or defend a legal claim. ”You need to think about how you will restrict processing in your organisation and have a clear procedure for what you will do. The easiest way of doing this will depend on who you keep information.

For example, if you mainly have paper records it might be easiest to remove the specific record and store it separately, in a locked drawer or similar for the duration of the restriction. If you have digital records, you might want to move the data temporarily to a restricted drive or remove access to the file for the duration of the restriction.

The right to data portability

This means that individuals have the right to be given the personal data which they given to you in a “structured, commonly used and machine readable format”. They can only ask for this is if your legal basis for processing this information is consent or for the performance of a contact and you hold the information digitally

and machine readable format”. They can only ask for this is if your legal basis for processing this information is consent or for the performance of a contact and you hold the information digitally

It is unlikely that this will happen in health care so there is not extensive guidance on this

The right to object

In certain instances, people can object to you processing their data. They can object to their data being used for direct marketing at any time and you must stop immediately – there are no exceptions to this.

They can also object if you are relying on public task or legitimate interests as your legal basis for processing. The individual must give you specific reasons why they object. You can decide to continue processing if you can demonstrate that you have compelling legitimate grounds for processing which overrides the interests, rights and freedoms of the individual or if the processing is necessary for a legal claim.

Staff Screening & Immunisation Policy

Data Protection Act 2018

The Practice will treat personal data collected during the process in accordance with its Data Protection Policy on processing special categories of personal data. Information about how an employee's data is used and the basis for processing his or her data will be provided in the Practice’s Employee Privacy Notice. Where the organisation is relying on its legitimate interests as the legal ground for processing an employee's data, he/she can object to the processing.

Introduction

This document covers the following immunisations for communicable diseases that are most likely to be present in a practice environment.

Hepatitis B

Tuberculosis (TB)

Measles Mumps and Rubella

Chicken Pox – (Varicella)

Flu Vaccination

Diphtheria

Tetanus

Poliomyelitis (Polio)

Coronavirus (Covid)

The Practice has a responsibility to do all that is reasonably practicable to reduce the risk of infection to employees, patients, visitors, and healthcare workers. The duty of care extends towards patients which includes taking reasonable precautions to protect them from communicable diseases. This data should be kept in line with the practice employee privacy notice.

of care extends towards patients which includes taking reasonable precautions to protect them from communicable diseases. This data should be kept in line with the practice employee privacy notice.

The policy sets out the responsibilities of the employee as well as the employer regarding screening and immunisation. Immunisation is important and must be carried out in order to:

Protect the individual and their family

Protect patients from exposure (especially vulnerable/immune-suppressed individuals)

Protect other healthcare staff from infection

Allow for efficient running of practice services without disruption.

The policy sets out duties and responsibilities of Practice Management, the Occupational Health Department (OHD) and the individual staff member, with regards to health screening and immunisation.

Practices must show that there is an effective staff immunisation protocol in place. It also details the vaccinations required for staff.  This policy relates to CQC Regulation 12 (Safe Care and Treatment).

Purpose

The purposes of this policy are to ensure that:

All staff who have or shall have direct contact with users of the service are protected in accordance with current national guidance and as advised by the OHD; and

of this policy are to ensure that:

All staff who have or shall have direct contact with users of the service are protected in accordance with current national guidance and as advised by the OHD; and

To restrict any health care workers infected with infectious diseases within the workplace where their infection may pose a risk to users of service in their care or other employees.

Pre-employment screening

The process for pre-employment screening of infectious diseases and the required vaccination programme is described. In summary, all new employees should undergo a pre-employment health assessment that includes a review of immunisation requirements. The Practice COSHH risk assessment will indicate the highest exposure risks in the workplace and those employees considered at risk of exposure to pathogens should be offered the appropriate routine pre-exposure immunisation.

will indicate the highest exposure risks in the workplace and those employees considered at risk of exposure to pathogens should be offered the appropriate routine pre-exposure immunisation.

Healthcare workers from outside the UK applying for a position or a training place must have standard clearance for serious communicable diseases. It is recommended that both standard and additional checks for serious communicable diseases be done in their own country before they apply for employment in the UK and provide the results in their health declaration to ensure they are aware of their professional responsibilities relating to preventing exposure to serious communicable diseases.

The Practice should make clear to applicants that any offers of employment is conditional on evidence of satisfactory health check clearance.

Duties & Responsibilities

The Practice Manager or Line Managers are responsible for ensuring their employees comply with this policy. Practices must ensure that all staff receive immunisations that are appropriate for their role.

Practice Manager or Line Managers are responsible for ensuring their employees comply with this policy. Practices must ensure that all staff receive immunisations that are appropriate for their role.

Where employees refuse to comply with the policy, Line Managers will determine what action is required, including the possibility of disciplinary action or even dismissal in the case of an unreasonable refusal to co-operate.

Where an individual is not protected by immunisation the Practice Manager is responsible for deploying the employee in such a way as to minimise the risk to the employee and / or to users of service and other staff. A risk assessment on the employee and job role should be done.

Occupational Health Department

The Practice must demonstrate an effective staff immunisation programme is in place and available as well as an obligation to arrange for this service at no cost to its staff. The OHD is responsible for providing a clinical screening and immunisation service and providing Practice Management with information regarding the immunisation status of their employees.

Existing Employees

Existing employees are expected to co-operate with this policy and to submit to screening and immunisation unless advised otherwise by the OHD.

status of their employees.

Existing Employees

Existing employees are expected to co-operate with this policy and to submit to screening and immunisation unless advised otherwise by the OHD.

New Employees

All staff new to the Practice must be referred to the OHD, which will be responsible for ensuring that appropriate pre-employment screening and immunisation procedures are followed in line with national guidance.

***Insert Name & Position of Person*** will be informed if employees do not attend for screening or immunisations – further details are available in the “Access to Occupational Health Protocol”, as an assessment form will need to be completed.

Recommendations for staff involved in direct patient care

All staff should be up to date with their routine immunisations, e.g.

Tetanus

Diphtheria

Polio

MMR (measles, mumps and rubella)

The MMR vaccine is especially important because of its ability to transmit measles or rubella infections to vulnerable groups. Healthcare workers should also have immunisation against measles and rubella in order to protect patients.

Practices must show satisfactory evidence of protection, including documentation of staff members receiving two doses of MMR or had a positive antibody tests for measles and rubella.

patients.

Practices must show satisfactory evidence of protection, including documentation of staff members receiving two doses of MMR or had a positive antibody tests for measles and rubella.

Immunisation

Immunisation to minimize the risk of cross infection within the workplace is required.

Hepatitis B

(Refer to “Hepatitis B Policy”)

All health care workers, including all Nursing, Clinical and Domestic Staff and Porters who may be exposed to blood or body fluids are strongly advised to be immunised against Hepatitis B, which can be undertaken by the OHC. This will be in line with the guidance issued by the Department of Health in the Green Book.

Immunisation is needed if they;

Have direct contact with patient’s blood or blood-stained body fluids, such as from sharps

are at direct risk of being injured by patients

Immunisation against Hepatitis B consists of three doses of vaccine at 0, 1 and 2 months. A single booster dose of vaccine at 5 years is recommended with no further booster required on a routine basis.

against Hepatitis B consists of three doses of vaccine at 0, 1 and 2 months. A single booster dose of vaccine at 5 years is recommended with no further booster required on a routine basis.

If the staff member cannot have the vaccination or declines immunisation, then a risk assessment of the staff member’s role and work area will be undertaken by ***Insert Name & Position of Person*** and the risks discussed with the individual.

Tuberculosis (TB)

All employees are required to be immunised or have immunity against tuberculosis (TB).

If the employee has no evidence of a scar, or BCG vaccination, then an appointment will need to be arranged with the Chest Clinic for a Mantoux Test, and if required, a BCG vaccination will be arranged.

The OHD will need to be advised of the result. A copy of their immunisation record will be forwarded to the OHD by ***Insert Name & Position of Person***.

If the staff member cannot have the vaccination or declines immunisation, then a risk assessment of the staff member’s role and work area will be needed and ***Insert Name & Position of Person*** will be informed of any restrictions that may be needed to be put into place.

then a risk assessment of the staff member’s role and work area will be needed and ***Insert Name & Position of Person*** will be informed of any restrictions that may be needed to be put into place.

Advice will be given to the individual regarding the signs and symptoms of TB by the person providing the immunisation.

Measles Mumps and Rubella

Documented evidence of an employee’s Measles, Mumps and Rubella antibody status or immunisation details is required.

Evidence of protection would include proof of receiving two doses of vaccines that cover Measles and Rubella (e.g. the MMR vaccine), or a positive antibody test for measles and rubella. This should be verified by a healthcare professional (preferably a GP). Unless there is a reliable history of immunisation, it should be assumed that an individual is not immunised, and the appropriate recommendations are followed.

If there is no documented evidence available then vaccination or a blood test will be undertaken.

If the staff member declines immunisation or blood test then ***Insert Name & Position of Person*** will need to be informed.

A risk assessment will need to be undertaken by ***Insert Name & Position of Person*** as to whether the staff member can work or continue to work in that area. The risk assessment will be dependent on where the employee actually works and whether patients and staff are being put at risk.

Chicken Pox – (Varicella)

Chicken pox is a common childhood illness in the UK. All employees that have direct patient contact will be asked if they have had Chicken Pox. Evidence of immunity to chicken pox would include;

History of chicken pox and shingles infection

Evidence of immunity by blood test

Vaccination against varicella (2 doses of vaccines 3 months apart)

For those employees who are unsure, blood testing will be undertaken. If after the blood test, chicken pox antibodies are negative, then immunisation will be recommended.

If the staff member declines immunisation or blood test then ***Insert Name & Position of Person***will need to be informed.

If the staff member declines immunisation or blood test then ***Insert Name & Position of Person***will need to be informed.

A risk assessment will need to be undertaken by ***Insert Name & Position of Person*** as to whether the staff member can work or continue to work in that area. The risk assessment will be dependent on where the employee actually works and whether patients and staff are being put at risk.

Flu Vaccination

All staff involved in the delivery of care and support to patients will be offered the annual flu vaccination at the commencement of the flu vaccination campaign and in line with Department of Health guidance.

The Department of Health recommends annual influenza vaccination of all clinical healthcare workers to protect them from contracting flu as a result of caring for patients as well as reducing the likelihood of illness and any related sickness absence from duties.

If the staff member declines immunisation or blood test then ***Insert Name & Position of Person*** will need to be informed.

Immunisation Records

and any related sickness absence from duties.

If the staff member declines immunisation or blood test then ***Insert Name & Position of Person*** will need to be informed.

Immunisation Records

***Insert Name & Position of Person*** will request all clinical staff to complete an immunisation record and this will then be forwarded to the Occupational Health Department who is responsible for keeping accurate immunisation records on all employees at the Practice. (A copy can also be maintained within the Practice as long as confidentiality is maintained at all times.)

Diphtheria

The main vaccinations against diphtheria are usually completed in infancy along with the polio vaccinations. The majority of people will have been immunised in childhood and diphtheria remains rare in the UK, however it is still common in some parts of the world. Vaccination may be advisable if there is no evidence of it, and if employees are from (or have worked in) areas where diphtheria is prevalent.

however it is still common in some parts of the world. Vaccination may be advisable if there is no evidence of it, and if employees are from (or have worked in) areas where diphtheria is prevalent.

Staff should be up to date with their immunisations – any staff that have not received a primary vaccination are required to have a full course. If a booster vaccine is given then they should attend 3 months later for repeat blood tests to confirm immunity and repeat the boosters at 10-year intervals.

Tetanus

Most UK citizens will have had a tetanus immunisation in childhood with a follow-up dose during their teens. Five doses of the vaccine in a lifetime are considered satisfactory long-term protection. Those staff who are in occupations which expose them to tetanus will be offered immunisations to complete the recommended course.

Poliomyelitis (Polio)

All staff should have received three doses of polio and two follow-up booster doses at 5 and 15 years following completion of primary course. Those who have not received this will be immunised with Tetanus/Diphtheria/ Inactivated Polio Vaccine (Td/IPV, sometimes referred to as DPT).

Coronavirus (COVID-19)

completion of primary course. Those who have not received this will be immunised with Tetanus/Diphtheria/ Inactivated Polio Vaccine (Td/IPV, sometimes referred to as DPT).

Coronavirus (COVID-19)

With the recent pandemic it is imperative that the surgery acts to minimise any risk to staff and others and where appropriate a vaccine is offered to all staff as well as a record being kept.

Where a staff member refuses to have such a vaccination then a reason and discussion should be had between the staff member and a clinical partner to ascertain the reason why.

After that, a decision must be made after taking a risk assessment as to how the staff member can be gainfully employed and with the minimum of risk.

It may be that this cannot happen, and that employment may need to be terminated after investigations have taken place and professional advice sought.

In the case of Lateral Flow tests available for staff to take, it is advisable to give each member off staff a box of tests to be done on set days at home before work ( twice a week, for example).  Results should be recorded by the practice.
