11.They can also refer onwards to orthopaedics, rheumatology and neurology as appropriate.

Referral Protocol & Additional Guidance

At various times, patients will need to be referred for a consultant opinion, operation, or procedure, e.g. x-ray, MRI etc., to another service or to another clinician within the practice.

When this happens, the Referrer should ensure the following takes place and is recorded accurately in the patient record ( you may wish to include Read Codes for various entries so that you are able to undertake searches at a later date for audit purposes - templates within the clinical system can be devised and used to ensure consistency and accuracy).

Where abuse is suspected, the patient and / or their carer should be advised about safeguarding arrangements, how they work and what other agencies may be involved:

Discussion regarding the need to refer and positive / negative outcomes.

The choices that the patient has about where to go for treatment.

Where the patient indicates that they would prefer treatment or investigations carried out privately, explain there will be costs attached to this option (payable to the private provider) and that they will be responsible for these, but that no charge will be levied by the Practice for writing the referral;

Patient’s preference on where to go.

How Choice and Booking works.

provider) and that they will be responsible for these, but that no charge will be levied by the Practice for writing the referral;

Patient’s preference on where to go.

How Choice and Booking works.

Whether the patient agrees with the management plan.

What the timescale is likely to be from referral to appointment.

Ask if the patient has any specific needs that the service being referred to needs to be aware of, e.g. carer, social issues, disability, advance directives etc.

If the patient is a military veteran and the problem is directly connected to their service, ensure this is noted in the referral (see Military Veterans - Protocol for Priority Treatment).

Where patients require diagnostic tests, including imaging, explain what they are and why they are needed together with how they should access the results.

Test that the patient has understood what they have been told and invite questions.

Allow the patient time to reflect on what has been discussed and if they choose not to go ahead with the referral, respect their decision.

Take care when dictating referrals and do this as soon as possible after the consultation, ideally within two working days from the appointment.

Letters should be checked for accuracy.

Include patient name, address, date of birth, NHS number, telephone number, past medical history, current medication together with any allergies or sensitivities, details of any investigations or tests that have been undertaken including the results.

Include information if the patient poses a risk to themselves or others, e.g. infection, suicide, temperament etc.

If a child or young person being referred for specialist healthcare is suspected to be at risk of, or suffering, abuse, or neglect, you should make sure that you include relevant information about the child protection concerns in the referral.

If the referral is delayed for any reason, the patient should be notified asap.

The referring clinician will be responsible for the continuing care of the patient and will act as the point of contact if there are any issues arising out of the refer

Please use the dedicated CCEG documents in Emis to refer or Dr Goel’s template letter for general referrals (See FY2 induction information). Remember to call the document the correct name,  eg “Homerton consultant and specialty” and code “refer to specialty” for audit purposes.

Peer Review of prospective referrals

Junior staff, AARS staff and locums are reminded to discuss or task Dr Goel or in his absence Dr Aggarwal before a routine referral. This is part of our local CCG contract to ensure that local care pathways where available are being used.

Please add the code “peer review of prospective referral carried out” to code these and allow audit of this activity.

Advice and Guidance

To help manage patients there are a number of advice and guidance services locally on ERS and some by direct phone advice to Homerton. These are found at the following webpage:

https://gps.cityandhackneyccg.nhs.uk/service/advice-and-guidance

Please add the code “consultant advice service used”

by direct phone advice to Homerton. These are found at the following webpage:

https://gps.cityandhackneyccg.nhs.uk/service/advice-and-guidance

Please add the code “consultant advice service used”

Remember to check for consultant replies on ERS every few days and to copy the request and guidance and save in the EMIS record.

Also to end the conversation on ERS.

2-week referrals

If making a 2-week referral always use the specific 2-week referral forms in EMIS. Always check that the symptoms meet the 2-week criteria

Complete the referral letter within 24 hours and ideally the same day.

Send an urgent task to the reception group, who will book the appointment on ERS.

Always confirm with the patient the 2-week referral and your concerns and that the appointment may be by phone.

Ask the patient to contact the surgery in 2 days if no appointment has been received from the hospital.

To safety net referrals, the referral document is coded automatically, and reception should also add the referral on the dedicated 2 week admin template with a diary date of 2 weeks.

Dr Goel runs the CEG 2 week search 2 weekly to track referrals and informs the practice manager if any actions are identified.

In his absence the practice manager runs this search and actions.

It will also help to establish a base-line view of the patient’s lifestyle and will assist the nurse/doctor in carrying out a new patient health check. The information provided will also assist in the identification of “at risk” patients and focus care advice on at-risk areas.

The Duty Doctor service provides GP telephone advice to patients within 2 hours for all urgent requests and responds to all urgent requests from health and social care professionals within 30 minutes.  Where possible, urgent calls from acute services (A&E and the London Ambulance Service should be responded to immediately).

The Duty Doctor is on call from 08:00 – 18:30 Monday – Friday (excluding bank holidays)

Health & social care professionals with urgent requests should contact the Duty Doctor via the bypass number 020 3962 4521.   The IPC will be notified of any changes to this number via email to: nelondonicb.gpcontracts@nhs.net

Examples of urgent calls from patients include, but are not restricted to:

Symptoms of sepsis

Pregnant and abdominal pain or bleeding

Chest pain

Suspected stroke

Monitoring

As part of any staff training the duty doctor protocol and response times are explained so that staff know when and how to contact the duty doctor as soon as they receive an urgent patient or health social care call. To avoid any delays these are put through straight away to the duty doctor who then can advise.

The response times are randomly checked by auditing duty doctor calls booked in the dedicated pink slots to ensure the response times and protocol are working and to identify any staff training needs.

Staff Training

As part of any new staff, temporary staff  and locum induction, the duty doctor protocol, pink slots and response times are explained so that there is no delay in responding to urgent calls.

Practice Closure/ Protected Learning Time (PLT)

The Duty Doctor service remains operational during any practice closures/training/PLT etc. There will be a receptionist and Duty Doctor available during PLT to ensure all urgent patient and professional calls are managed and responded to within the timeframes.

The practice adheres to the IPC PLT guidance available on the IPC website.

The Riverside Practice Health Inequality Policy

Practice Name: The Riverside Practice

Date Adopted: 19.9.2025

Review Date: 19.9.2026

Lead for Health Inequalities: Dr R Goel-Lead GP

1. Purpose and Statement of Commitment

We recognise that health inequalities are avoidable, unfair, and systemic differences in health outcomes across population groups. As a GP practice, we are committed to:

Reducing barriers to access

Providing equitable, person-centred care

Improving health outcomes for underserved groups in our community

This policy sets out our approach to embedding health equity into our practice operations, services, and quality improvement.

2. Understanding Our Population

We use local data to understand the characteristics of our patient population, including:

Levels of deprivation (e.g. IMD quintiles)

Ethnic and cultural diversity

Language and literacy needs

Inclusion health groups (e.g. people experiencing homelessness, refugees, Gypsy, Roma & Traveller populations)

Long-term conditions and multimorbidity prevalence

3. Access and Inclusion

We aim to remove barriers to care through:

Accessible appointments: Extended hours, same-day slots, telephone/online options

Language support: Access to interpreters and translated materials

aim to remove barriers to care through:

Accessible appointments: Extended hours, same-day slots, telephone/online options

Language support: Access to interpreters and translated materials

Registration without ID/address in line with NHS guidance

Digital inclusion: Alternative booking for patients who struggle with online systems

4. Cultural Competency and Staff Awareness

We commit to:

Providing staff training on health inequalities, unconscious bias, and cultural competence

Promoting inclusive communication, including for neurodiverse and learning-disabled patients

Respecting patients’ cultural and religious needs during care delivery

5. Service Design and Delivery

We tailor services to address known inequities:

Targeted outreach for screening and immunisation (e.g. cervical, flu, COVID-19)

Extended reviews for patients with multiple LTCs or social vulnerabilities

Working with social prescribers and link workers to address wider determinants

Partnership with voluntary/community sector organisations

6. Monitoring and Evaluation

We monitor and review equity using:

Clinical and access data disaggregated by age, sex, ethnicity, and deprivation

Patient feedback from diverse groups

Outcome tracking for targeted interventions

monitor and review equity using:

Clinical and access data disaggregated by age, sex, ethnicity, and deprivation

Patient feedback from diverse groups

Outcome tracking for targeted interventions

Annual audit of equity in screening, prescribing, and referrals

Findings are discussed at clinical and management meetings, with QI cycles applied where needed.

7. Patient and Community Engagement

We involve patients and the community by:

Actively recruiting underrepresented groups to our Patient Participation Group (PPG)

Co-producing outreach initiatives with community partners

Conducting accessible surveys and listening exercises

8. Partnership Working

We collaborate with:

Local PCNs and ICB on other equity initiatives

Local authority public health and social care teams

Third sector organisations working with marginalised groups

9. Review and Accountability

This policy is reviewed annually and updated in line with best practice guidance from NHS England, RCGP, and CQC.

The practice’s Health Inequalities Lead is responsible for overseeing the delivery and monitoring of this policy.

The Riverside Practice

Standard Operating Procedure for Prescribing a Controlled Drug and the Collection of the Prescription

Objectives

To ensure prescriptions issued for controlled drugs (CDs) are legal, accurate and conform to good practice and there is a process in place for their collection.

Scope

This SOP encompasses all prescriptions produced for controlled drugs within the Practice.

Responsibilities

The authorised clinician is responsible for all clinical and legal aspects of prescribing of controlled drugs in accordance with the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971 and the Human Medicines Regulation 2012.

CDs should be prescribed according to national and local guidance. Prescribers should be able to justify ‘outlying’ prescribing.

Authorised practice staff may be responsible for generating prescriptions.

The Practice manager should keep a full list of the prescribers and their scope of prescribing authority. A record of an annual review should be kept of the prescribing authority of clinicians from their professional body’s website.

Practice staff are responsible for ensuring there is an audit trail for the collection of controlled drug prescriptions.

Process and Records

docTitle: Riverside Practice SOP for Prescribing a Controlled Drug and the Collectio

source.path: rag/sources/Riverside Practice SOP for Prescribing a Controlled Drug and the Collectio.docx

text:
of clinicians from their professional body’s website.

Practice staff are responsible for ensuring there is an audit trail for the collection of controlled drug prescriptions.

Process and Records

The need for a CD prescription is identified and use of non-pharmacological treatment and non-CDs has been considered.

Only those prescribers legally able to prescribe controlled drugs may prescribe within their own competence.

All prescribers should be familiar with the security processes of controlled drug prescriptions. Further guidance can be found in Management of Prescriptions Forms; A guide for prescribers and health organisations.

Prescriptions for Schedule 2, 3 and 4 CDs are only valid for 28 days. The 28 day period of validity runs from the date the prescription was signed unless the prescriber has specified a start date on the prescription. For instalment dispensing prescriptions, the first supply must be made within 28 days of the appropriate date and the remainder of the instalments must be dispensed only in accordance with the directions on the prescription.

Prescribing of controlled drugs should only be as an acute item in order to monitor use and allow medication reviews and a maximum of a 30 day supply.

docTitle: Riverside Practice SOP for Prescribing a Controlled Drug and the Collectio

source.path: rag/sources/Riverside Practice SOP for Prescribing a Controlled Drug and the Collectio.docx

text:
with the directions on the prescription.

Prescribing of controlled drugs should only be as an acute item in order to monitor use and allow medication reviews and a maximum of a 30 day supply.

There is a good practice requirement that the quantity of Schedules 2, 3 and 4 CDs be limited to a quantity for up to 30 days treatment. In cases where the prescribed believes that a prescription should be issued for a longer period they may do so, but will need to be able to justify that there is a clinical need and that it would not cause an unacceptable risk to patient safety. This information should be clearly documented in the patient’s notes.

The generated prescription (either FP10 or private prescription FP10PCD) must be completed on the appropriate form and in accordance with the legal requirements for the relevant schedule. Information  that  must  be  included  on both  NHS  and private prescriptions forms for controlled drugs in Schedules 2 and 3 is:

The patient’s full name, address, age, date of birth and (where possible) their NHS number. Legally an age is required if below 12 years but it is good practice to include the date of birth for all prescriptions.

The name, form and strength of the drug.

docTitle: Riverside Practice SOP for Prescribing a Controlled Drug and the Collectio

source.path: rag/sources/Riverside Practice SOP for Prescribing a Controlled Drug and the Collectio.docx

text:
(where possible) their NHS number. Legally an age is required if below 12 years but it is good practice to include the date of birth for all prescriptions.

The name, form and strength of the drug.

The dose and frequency to be taken (‘as directed’ etc. is NOT legal).

The total quantity of the preparation in both words and figures.

The prescriber’s name and Practice address must be stated on the prescription, together with their registration number and profession.

The prescription should be signed and dated by the prescriber in indelible ink and include the prescriber’s UK address (and contact details). An Advanced Electronic Signature is acceptable for EPS2 prescriptions where S2 and S3 CDs are able to be prescribed electronically. The prescriber’s name should be legible.

A prescriber code is necessary for NHSBSA and for private prescriptions.

Independent and Supplementary prescribers must also include their designation, professional registration number and prescriber code.

Schedule 2 and Schedule 3 CDs cannot be prescribed on the repeat dispensing service (RDS). The first supply of Schedule 4 CDs on RDS must be within 28 days of issue and for S5 CDs within 6 months of issue; after then the prescription is valid for 12 months from the date of issue.

docTitle: Riverside Practice SOP for Prescribing a Controlled Drug and the Collectio

source.path: rag/sources/Riverside Practice SOP for Prescribing a Controlled Drug and the Collectio.docx

text:
(RDS). The first supply of Schedule 4 CDs on RDS must be within 28 days of issue and for S5 CDs within 6 months of issue; after then the prescription is valid for 12 months from the date of issue.

If a prescriber makes a home visit and a controlled drug is prescribed, the patient`s medication record should be updated at the earliest opportunity (ideally 48 hours).

The identity and address of a patient collecting a prescription must be checked. The collection of a controlled drug prescription must also be recorded in a bound book specifically for this use and include the patient’s name, controlled drug, name of person collecting the drug, their signature and date of collection: All CD prescriptions should be signed for when collected from the Practice, whether this is a healthcare professional, community pharmacy staff, the patient or their representative.

If a prescription for a CD is lost every attempt should be made to locate it and the prescriber should be notified along with the NHS England CD Accountable Officer. Refer to the SOP for dealing with Lost/Stolen/Forged Prescriptions.

docTitle: Riverside Practice SOP for Prescribing a Controlled Drug and the Collectio

source.path: rag/sources/Riverside Practice SOP for Prescribing a Controlled Drug and the Collectio.docx

text:
every attempt should be made to locate it and the prescriber should be notified along with the NHS England CD Accountable Officer. Refer to the SOP for dealing with Lost/Stolen/Forged Prescriptions.

Patients / carers should have clear information on how to safely use, store and dispose the prescribed CD; document when the drug driving advice is given. The patient / carer should be informed that identity evidence will be required when collecting dispensed schedule 2 and 3 CDs.

High-dose prescribing should be with reference to appropriate specialist advice. Prolonged prescribing of high doses should involve review by an alternative prescriber within the practice.

For patients travelling abroad, prescribers should be aware of the advice from the Home Office at https://www.gov.uk/travelling-controlled-drugs

CH 20 CH 20 Safe management of blood and body fluid spillages July 2023 Version 2.01 Community IPC Policy for Care Home settings Page 4 of 9 © Harrogate and District NHS Foundation Trust SAFE MANAGEMENT OF BLOOD AND BODY FLUID SPILLAGES SAFE MANAGEMENT OF BLOOD AND BODY FLUID SPILLAGES 1. Introduction This Policy is one of the ‘Standard infection control precautions’ (SICPs) referred to by NHS England in the National infection prevention and control manual (NIPCM) for England . Blood and body fluids, e.g. urine and faeces, may contain a large number of microorganisms, such as bacteria and viruses. Staff who may have contact with blood or blood stained body fluids, or are exposed to sharps or other exposure risks, should have the opportunity for hepatitis B vaccination and antibody testing to check for their response. Contamination or spillages with blood or body fluids should be dealt with immediately, as this may expose staff and others to infection. Blood and body fluid spillages should be managed by staff trained in the correct procedure. Always use SICPs and, where required, ‘Transmission based precautions’ (TBPs), refer to the ‘SICPs and TBPs Policy for Care Home Settings’.

When caring for residents in relation to COVID-19 or any other new emerging infections, staff should refer to national infection prevention and control guidance. 2. Dealing with blood and body fluid spillages Clean up blood and body fluid spillage promptly to reduce the risk of infection to other people. Appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) should be worn, e.g. non- sterile disposable gloves and apron, and SICPs followed. Best practice is to use an appropriate spillage kit for the type of spillage, following the manufacturer’s guidance and ensuring it is within the expiry date. Some spillage kits are suitable for all types of body fluids, including blood, e.g. spill wipes, always check the manufacturer’s instructions on suitability and usage. Spillage kits may contain solidifying polymer granules. A National Resident Safety Alert issued in 2017, following a number of deaths and incidents related to ingestion of the product, advises a risk assessment and procedures in place to ensure supplies are securely stored away from the general public.

CH 20 Community IPC Policy for Care Home settings CH 20 Safe management of blood and body fluid spillages July 2023 Version 2.01 © Harrogate and District NHS Foundation Trust Page 5 of 9 SAFE MANAGEMENT OF BLOOD AND BODY FLUID SPILLAGES In the absence of spillage kits, chlorine-based solutions can be prepared and used as in the Tables below: • The required dilution for dealing with blood or blood stained body fluid spillages is 10,000 ppm (parts per million) available chlorine, or equivalent product as per manufacturer’s instructions • The required dilution for dealing with body fluid spillages that are not blood or blood stained, is 1,000 ppm available chlorine, or equivalent product as per manufacturer’s instructions Action for blood and/or blood stained body fluid spillages Dilution of 10,000 ppm (parts per million) available chlorine 1. Clean hands and put on disposable apron and gloves (wear facial protection if there is a risk of splashing). 2. Ventilate the area, e.g. open windows/doors, as fumes will be released when using chlorine. 3. Place disposable paper towels over the spillage to absorb and contain it, then apply the solution to the towels. Leave for the required contact time as specified by manufacturer. 4.

Clear away the spillage and dispose of as infectious waste. 5. With detergent wipes or general purpose neutral detergent and warm water and disposable cloth, clean the area, then leave to air dry or dry with paper towels. 6. Dispose of cloth and paper towels as infectious waste. 7. Remove and dispose of PPE as infectious waste. 8. Wash hands with liquid soap and warm running water, rinse and dry thoroughly to prevent the transmission of infection. Action for body fluid spillages Dilution of 1,000 ppm available chlorine 1. Do not use a solution containing chlorine directly on to urine as toxic fumes will be released. 2. Clean hands and put on disposable apron and gloves (wear facial protection if there is a risk of splashing). 3. Ventilate the area, e.g. open windows/doors, as fumes will be released when using chlorine. 4. Soak up any excess liquid/clean up any solid material using paper towels. If it is a urine spillage, a gelling agent can be used. Place solution directly onto the spillage. Leave for the required contact time as specified by the manufacturer. 5. Clear away the spillage and dispose of as infectious or offensive waste. 6.
With detergent wipes or general purpose neutral detergent and warm water and disposable cloth, clean the area, then leave to air dry or dry with paper towels.

CH 20 CH 20 Safe management of blood and body fluid spillages July 2023 Version 2.01 Community IPC Policy for Care Home settings Page 6 of 9 © Harrogate and District NHS Foundation Trust SAFE MANAGEMENT OF BLOOD AND BODY FLUID SPILLAGES • Dispose of waste and PPE as infectious or offensive waste. • Wash hands with liquid soap and warm running water, rinse and dry thoroughly with paper towels. • If a mop and bucket are used, they should be in accordance with the national colour coding, refer to the ‘Safe management of the care environment Policy for Care Home settings’. After use, the mop head should be disposed of immediately as infectious or offensive waste and the bucket washed with general purpose neutral detergent and warm water and dried with paper towels, and then wiped with a chlorine-based disinfectant at 1,000 ppm available chlorine, or equivalent product as per manufacturer’s instructions, and stored upside down to air dry. • All cloths must be single use and disposed of after use. 3. Use of chlorine-based disinfectants • Always use the appropriate PPE, e.g. disposable apron and gloves, and wear facial protection if there is a risk of splashing to the face.

• Some disinfectants supplied as tablets must be made up with the specified amount of water using a diluter bottle in order to achieve the correct concentration. • Always use cold water when diluting chlorine-based disinfectants. • If the dilution of the chlorine-based disinfectant is incorrect and a weak solution is used, any blood-borne virus, e.g. hepatitis B, hepatitis C and HIV, will not be killed. If the dilution is too strong, the equipment or surfaces may be damaged. • Diluted chlorine-based disinfectant solutions become less effective after 24 hours. When a solution is made, the date and time should be recorded and the solution disposed of after 24 hours. • To ensure that microorganisms, such as bacteria and viruses are killed, always leave chlorine-based disinfectant solutions for 5-10 minutes contact time or as specified by the manufacturer. • Do not use a chlorine-based disinfectant solution directly on urine as toxic fumes will be released. • Chlorine-based disinfectants, such as household bleach, should not be used on soft furnishings, untreated wood and carpets as it will cause ‘whitening/ Action for body fluid spillages Dilution of 1,000 ppm available chlorine 7. Dispose of cloth and paper towels as infectious or offensive waste. 8.
Remove and dispose of PPE as infectious or offensive waste. 9. Wash hands with liquid soap and warm running water, rinse and dry thoroughly to prevent the transmission of infection.

Therefore, only general purpose neutral detergent and warm water, a carpet shampoo machine or steam cleaner, should be used. • If soft furnishings or other items are heavily contaminated with blood or body fluids that cannot be adequately decontaminated, they should be disposed of. 4. Infection Prevention and Control resources, education and training See Appendix 1 for the ‘Safe management of blood and body fluid spillages : Quick reference guide’. The Community Infection Prevention and Control (IPC) Team have produced a wide range of innovative educational and IPC resources designed to assist your Care Home in achieving compliance with the Health and Social Care Act 2008: code of practice on the prevention and control of infections and related resources and CQC registration requirements.

These resources are either free to download from the website or available at a minimal cost covering administration and printing: • 30 IPC Policy documents for Care Home settings • Preventing Infection Workbook: Guidance for Care Homes • IPC CQC inspection preparation Pack for Care Homes • IPC audit tools, posters, leaflets and factsheets • IPC Bulletin for Care Homes In addition, we hold IPC educational training events in North Yorkshire. Further information on these high quality evidence-based resources is available at www.infectionpreventioncontrol.co.uk . 5. docTitle: Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy

source.path: rag/sources/Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy.docx

text:
Riverside PracticeSexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy

Practice: Riverside PracticeEffective From: February 2026Review Date: March 2027Policy Owner: Iqra Khan – Practice ManagerApproved By: Dr R Goel – GP PartnerApplies To: All staff working at or on behalf of Riverside Practice

Content Warning

This policy addresses sexual misconduct and may be distressing for some individuals. Staff who require support should speak with the Practice Manager, Lead GP, or Freedom to Speak Up Guardian, or access appropriate wellbeing services.

1. Purpose

Riverside Practice is committed to providing a safe, respectful and inclusive workplace for staff, patients, visitors and contractors.

The practice has signed up to the NHS Sexual Safety Charter and adopts a zero-tolerance approach to sexual misconduct, sexual harassment, sexual violence and any inappropriate behaviour of a sexual nature.

docTitle: Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy

source.path: rag/sources/Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy.docx

text:
has signed up to the NHS Sexual Safety Charter and adopts a zero-tolerance approach to sexual misconduct, sexual harassment, sexual violence and any inappropriate behaviour of a sexual nature.

This policy outlines the practice’s commitment to sexual safety, defines sexual misconduct, explains how concerns or incidents can be reported, describes how reports will be managed, identifies the support available to those affected, and explains escalation routes including NHS England, the Care Quality Commission, safeguarding authorities, professional regulators and the police where appropriate.

This policy should be read alongside the practice’s Whistleblowing Policy, Disciplinary Policy, Grievance Policy, Safeguarding Policies, Chaperone Policy, Dignity at Work Policy, Staff Code of Conduct and Incident Reporting Policy.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all individuals working in or on behalf of Riverside Practice. This includes permanent staff, temporary staff, GP partners, locum clinicians, agency and bank staff, trainees, students, volunteers and contractors.

docTitle: Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy

source.path: rag/sources/Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy.docx

text:
working in or on behalf of Riverside Practice. This includes permanent staff, temporary staff, GP partners, locum clinicians, agency and bank staff, trainees, students, volunteers and contractors.

Sexual misconduct may occur on practice premises, during home visits, during training or work-related events, during travel for work, online or through digital communication, or between colleagues, staff and patients or third parties.

All concerns will be taken seriously regardless of who is involved.

3. Policy Statement

Riverside Practice maintains a zero-tolerance approach to sexual misconduct. The practice promotes a culture of openness, safety and respect. All staff are expected to behave professionally and respectfully at all times.

The practice will support anyone affected by sexual misconduct, respond promptly and fairly to reports, ensure staff know how to report concerns and ensure staff complete sexual safety training.

Victimisation or retaliation against any individual raising concerns will not be tolerated.

4. Definition of Sexual Misconduct

Sexual misconduct refers to unwanted behaviour of a sexual nature that causes or could cause distress, humiliation, intimidation, fear or harm.

It may be physical, verbal, visual, written or digital.

docTitle: Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy

source.path: rag/sources/Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy.docx

text:
misconduct refers to unwanted behaviour of a sexual nature that causes or could cause distress, humiliation, intimidation, fear or harm.

It may be physical, verbal, visual, written or digital.

Examples include sexual comments or jokes, inappropriate questions about someone’s body or sex life, sexual gestures or staring, sharing sexual images or messages, unwanted touching or physical contact, sexual propositions or coercion, stalking, harassment, sexual assault or abuse, or abuse of professional authority for sexual purposes.

A single incident may constitute sexual harassment.

5. Key Principles

The practice will manage concerns in accordance with the following principles: prioritising safety and wellbeing, maintaining dignity and respect, maintaining confidentiality on a need-to-know basis, conducting fair and impartial investigations, supporting all parties involved, protecting individuals from victimisation and recognising situations where power imbalance may exist.

6. Roles and Responsibilities

All staff must behave professionally and respectfully, complete required sexual safety training, challenge inappropriate behaviour where it is safe to do so, report concerns promptly and maintain confidentiality.

docTitle: Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy

source.path: rag/sources/Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy.docx

text:
behave professionally and respectfully, complete required sexual safety training, challenge inappropriate behaviour where it is safe to do so, report concerns promptly and maintain confidentiality.

The Practice Manager is responsible for receiving and documenting reports, ensuring appropriate support and safety measures are implemented, coordinating investigations where necessary and escalating concerns to external bodies where required.

The Lead GP and partners will support decision-making in serious cases, ensure patient safety and professional standards are maintained and assist with escalation to external organisations where necessary.

The Freedom to Speak Up Guardian provides an alternative confidential reporting route for staff who feel unable to raise concerns through management.

7. Reporting Sexual Misconduct

Concerns may be reported to the Practice Manager, Lead GP, Freedom to Speak Up Guardian, Safeguarding Lead where safeguarding concerns exist, or the police in emergencies.

If the concern involves senior staff, alternative reporting routes should be used.

8. Responding to a Disclosure

docTitle: Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy

source.path: rag/sources/Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy.docx

text:
Safeguarding Lead where safeguarding concerns exist, or the police in emergencies.

If the concern involves senior staff, alternative reporting routes should be used.

8. Responding to a Disclosure

Staff receiving a disclosure should listen calmly, ensure the individual is safe, take the concern seriously, avoid judgement or blame, explain confidentiality limits, document the concern, escalate the matter appropriately and signpost available support.

Staff should not attempt to investigate the matter themselves.

9. Immediate Safety Measures

Following a report the practice may implement safety measures such as separating individuals involved, adjusting work arrangements, restricting duties or contact, arranging supervision or support, requesting individuals leave the premises, temporary suspension where appropriate, or safeguarding or police referral where necessary.

10. Managing Reports

All reports will be reviewed promptly and may lead to fact-finding, formal investigation, disciplinary or grievance procedures, safeguarding referral, police referral, professional regulator referral or notification to NHS England or the Care Quality Commission where appropriate.

Reports will be taken seriously even where evidence is limited.

docTitle: Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy

source.path: rag/sources/Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy.docx

text:
police referral, professional regulator referral or notification to NHS England or the Care Quality Commission where appropriate.

Reports will be taken seriously even where evidence is limited.

11. Sexual Misconduct Involving Patients or the Public

Sexual misconduct may involve patients, relatives, visitors or contractors. Such incidents will be managed in line with the practice’s zero-tolerance approach, safeguarding policies and professional regulatory obligations where relevant.

12. Investigations

Investigations will be fair, proportionate and confidential. They will be conducted by appropriate management and documented securely. Staff involved in investigations may be accompanied where appropriate. External HR advice may be sought where necessary.

13. Disciplinary Outcomes

Sexual misconduct may constitute serious or gross misconduct. Outcomes may include formal warnings, removal of duties, termination of contract, dismissal, referral to professional regulators or referral to the police where appropriate.

14. External Reporting

docTitle: Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy

source.path: rag/sources/Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy.docx

text:
Outcomes may include formal warnings, removal of duties, termination of contract, dismissal, referral to professional regulators or referral to the police where appropriate.

14. External Reporting

Where necessary the practice may report concerns to NHS England, the Care Quality Commission, the General Medical Council, the Nursing and Midwifery Council, the Health and Care Professions Council, the Disclosure and Barring Service, safeguarding authorities or the police.

15. Support for Staff

The practice recognises that sexual misconduct may have a significant emotional impact. Support may include confidential discussions with management, Freedom to Speak Up support, occupational health or wellbeing support, temporary adjustments to duties or working arrangements and signposting to external support organisations.

16. Confidentiality

Information relating to reports will be shared only on a need-to-know basis. Confidentiality will be maintained wherever possible except where safeguarding concerns exist, where there is risk to others, or where legal or regulatory reporting is required.

17. Victimisation

docTitle: Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy

source.path: rag/sources/Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy.docx

text:
Confidentiality will be maintained wherever possible except where safeguarding concerns exist, where there is risk to others, or where legal or regulatory reporting is required.

17. Victimisation

Riverside Practice will not tolerate retaliation or victimisation against individuals who report concerns or act as witnesses. Reports made in good faith will not result in negative consequences even if allegations are not upheld. Deliberately false or malicious allegations may result in disciplinary action.

18. Training and Prevention

All staff are required to complete sexual safety awareness training. Sexual safety will be included in staff induction. The practice will promote awareness of reporting routes, complete the Sexual Safety Charter self-assessment checklist and review related policies including the Chaperone Policy.

19. Monitoring and Review

The Practice Manager and Lead GP will monitor incidents, training completion and lessons learned. This policy will be reviewed annually or sooner if national guidance changes.

20. Equality and Inclusion

docTitle: Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy

source.path: rag/sources/Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy.docx

text:
Practice Manager and Lead GP will monitor incidents, training completion and lessons learned. This policy will be reviewed annually or sooner if national guidance changes.

20. Equality and Inclusion

Riverside Practice recognises that sexual misconduct may disproportionately affect certain groups. The practice is committed to ensuring all staff feel safe to raise concerns regardless of background, role or identity.

Shared Care Policy

Introduction

1.1 – Policy Statement

To ensure that any shared are with other providers is safe and in line with local ICB and CCG protocols.

1.2 – Scope

This protocol applies to:

Sometimes requests are made for the GP surgery to take part in shared care such as investigations, prescribing and follow up, while still being under the care of the other provider for their condition.

Staff Data Security and Protection Code of Conduct – Guidance

Respecting the data rights of our service users and employees is of utmost importance to The Riverside Practice.

During your job you may be required to handle, collect, or share information of a sensitive nature. It is vital that our policies and processes are followed when you handle personal information. This will ensure that our staff and service user’s rights, dignity and wellbeing are promoted at all times.

You must not disclose, either during or after your employment here:

i. any confidential information, financial & staff information or;

ii. Other sensitive personal information or confidential information e.g. service user medical records & payroll details.

Except where this is necessary for your job or if you are required to do so by law.

We provide training to all staff on data security and protection and proper information sharing. If you feel that you require more training, or if you have any questions or concerns, please contact your line manager or insert most applicable person here.

and proper information sharing. If you feel that you require more training, or if you have any questions or concerns, please contact your line manager or insert most applicable person here.

So that we can make sure that confidential information is accessed by those individuals that have a legitimate right of access, we undertake monitoring on a regular basis. Audits are also carried out with a view to discover whether confidentiality has been breached. It is important that you know that if we discover that our policies and procedures have been breached, this may result in disciplinary action including dismissal.

Our Data Protection Champion or equivalent job role is Dr R Goel and he should be your first contact point if you have any questions or concerns.

Staff Guidance on Data Breaches

What is a data breach and a data incident?

A data breach or data incident is when the information we hold, create, or share (e.g. care records) is compromised. A data incident is a minor breach or a near miss. This might be in one of 3 ways:

Confidentiality

When a person gains access to information they shouldn’t have. This might be malicious i.e. a hacker, or it might be a simple mistake i.e. sending a fax to the wrong number;

Integrity

We need to know that information is accurate and that it was created by the right person. For example, a MAR sheet needs to have been filled out correctly. If there is an error on the sheet – whether on purpose or not – this is a data breach; or

Availability

For data to be useful we need to be able to access it. If it isn’t available this is also a breach, e.g. there is a care record which is needed to provide care for someone, this is kept locked in an office, if the keys go missing and no one can access that record then this is a data breach.

What should I do if there is a data breach or I think there is a data breach?

this is kept locked in an office, if the keys go missing and no one can access that record then this is a data breach.

What should I do if there is a data breach or I think there is a data breach?

It is better to report a breach even if you are not sure that it is one. As with incident reporting, near misses are as important to report as actual incidents. This is how we learn and can hopefully prevent these things happening in the future.

What to do:

If you believe a crime has been committed, someone has been injured, or an intruder is on site contact the emergency services via 999.

If you have noticed an issue or been involved in a potential or actual data breach or incident you must fill out a Data Security Incident Report/Significant Event  Form immediately and talk to the data security lead as below. These can be found in reception. This form should then be handed to the Data Protection lead, Senior Information Risk Officer (Practice Manager) or equivalent job role.

If you have identified a potential security breach, inform your line manager or at the earliest opportunity.

If you have identified a potential security breach, inform your line manager or at the earliest opportunity.

Your Data Protection leads will discuss the breach or incident with the practice’s Data Protection Officer to determine its severity. The incident, if serious, will be reported via the Data Security and  Protection toolkit and recorded as Significant Event and be included as part of the practice’s Significant Event process

docTitle: The Riverside Practice   Portable Heaters Safety Policy

source.path: rag/sources/The Riverside Practice - Portable Heaters Safety Policy.docx

text:
The Riverside Practice

Portable Heaters Safety Policy

Purpose & Scope

This policy governs the safe use, storage, and management of portable heaters within the practice. It aims to minimise fire, electrical, and physical hazards in alignment with UK health and safety standards.

Risk Assessment & Authorisation

Use of portable heaters requires prior risk assessment and approval from the Health & Safety Lead.

Placement & Installation

Heaters must be placed on stable, flat, non‑combustible surfaces.

Maintain a minimum clearance of 1 metre from furniture, curtains, clothing, papers, and other combustible materials.

Do not position heaters where they may be knocked over, obstruct escape routes, or where doors may open onto them.

Electrical Safety

Always plug heaters directly into a wall socket. Extension leads or multi‑way adaptors must be avoided.

Ensure the flex isn’t routed under doors or carpets and remains in good condition.

Operation & Monitoring

Heaters must not be left unattended or operated overnight. Always switch off and unplug at the end of the day or when not needed.

Never move a heater while it’s switched on or hot.

Do not dry clothes or place items on or near heaters.

Maintenance & Inspections

docTitle: The Riverside Practice   Portable Heaters Safety Policy

source.path: rag/sources/The Riverside Practice - Portable Heaters Safety Policy.docx

text:
switch off and unplug at the end of the day or when not needed.

Never move a heater while it’s switched on or hot.

Do not dry clothes or place items on or near heaters.

Maintenance & Inspections

Perform regular visual checks for damage, wear, or malfunction. Any faulty heaters must be removed from service immediately and tagged accordingly.

Clean heaters to remove dust and debris as per manufacturer’s guidance to reduce fire risk.

Fire Safety Preparedness

Allocate appropriate fire extinguishers nearby (e.g. CO₂ or water-based). Foam extinguishers must not be used on electrical heater fires.

Keep smoke detectors functional and routinely tested. Staff must be familiar with the evacuation procedures in the event of fire.

Training & Awareness

All staff must be briefed on this policy, especially when heaters are in use. Regular refresher training is required.

Heaters should be registered with the manufacturer to receive updates or recall notices.

Review

This policy will be reviewed annually or sooner if there are changes in legislation, building configuration, or following an incident.

docTitle: THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE 2 WEEK REFERRALS PROTOCOL

source.path: rag/sources/THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE 2 WEEK REFERRALS PROTOCOL.docx

text:
THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE 2 WEEK REFERRALS PROTOCOL

PURPOSE

To ensure that all suspected 2 week referrals made by the practice are followed up to ensure:

No delay in diagnosis

Identify any GP actions or follow up

Good clinical governance by keeping accurate patient records of any outcomes and diagnoses

Maintain accurate cancer and if applicable palliative care registers

Timely review of a patient with a new cancer diagnosis

Follow up of patients who do not attend their appointment

APPLICABILTY

All staff involved in a 2 week referral including admin and doctors.

PROCESS

Referrers must complete the suspected cancer 2 week template logging the type of cancer suspected and ensure admin are informed live at the appointment

Admin to try and book the patient appointment live while at the surgery and inform the patient of the date and time.

Admin to enter in the patient notes and template the date of the appointment

Admin to maintain a list of all referrals, date of appointment, confirm if clinic letter received

GPs to code on the 2 week template if cancer was confirmed, and if the patient attended

docTitle: The Riverside Practice Cancer Safety Netting Protocol

source.path: rag/sources/The Riverside Practice Cancer Safety Netting Protocol.docx

text:
The Riverside Practice

Cancer safety netting and bowel cancer screening protocol

The Riverside Practice is committed to the care and screening of patients at risk of cancer. The following is our protocol for cancer safety netting of all urgent 2ww referrals and bowel cancer screening:

Bowel cancer screening:

CEG Searches to be run monthly by cancer admin lead. This will identify those patients:

Due for bowel cancer screening invitation (ie turning 56 or 60)

All patients due to be sent screening kits to be telephoned and encouraged to return kits completed

If patients are not contactable by telephone a standard letter is to be sent

Patients not wanting to engage with screening to be coded

Those not eligible can be coded

Patients whose screening is incomplete/ spoiled/ non-response

All patients who have failed to return kits will be telephoned and encouraged to complete kits

If patients are not contactable by telephone letters will be sent

Patients not wanting to engage to be coded

Those not eligible are to be coded

New kits can be obtained by calling 0800 707 6060

Use the bowel cancer screening guide for a script to use when talking to patients. It also contains answers to frequently asked questions

docTitle: The Riverside Practice Cancer Safety Netting Protocol

source.path: rag/sources/The Riverside Practice Cancer Safety Netting Protocol.docx

text:
be coded

New kits can be obtained by calling 0800 707 6060

Use the bowel cancer screening guide for a script to use when talking to patients. It also contains answers to frequently asked questions

http://www.cityandhackneyccg.nhs.uk/gp/bowel-screening.htm

Use CEG Searches for identifying patients

Use CEG Template for recording the above activity

Cancer safety netting:

Cancer safety netting search and auto report to be run weekly.

All cancer referrals to be coded using template

All referrals to be made within 24 hours of patient’s attendance and coded on template.

Check that all referrals have received an appointment and that patients are contacted to confirm that they have received their appointment date and are going to attend.

Check to confirm patient attended their appointment when Hospital letter received and coded on template or DNA notification.  GP cancer leads to be informed of any patients who have not attended appointment.

Cancer template to be completed with cancer diagnosis confirmed  or coded as no cancer confirmed.

3 monthly auto reports for evidence to be run at end of reporting period and sent to confederation

Urgent Radiology results sent to the practice generic email inbox:

3 monthly auto reports for evidence to be run at end of reporting period and sent to confederation

Urgent Radiology results sent to the practice generic email inbox:

The practice generic email inbox is checked every 2 hours during the working day for any urgent emails including urgent radiology results.

These are printed out and given to the duty doctor for action and then scanned and added to the relevant EMIS patient record.

If appropriate this generates a 2 week urgent cancer referral and the patient is informed and given details of their hospital appointment once booked.

Welcome to The Riverside Practice! We aim to make your time here efficient, safe, and well-supported. This guide combines all necessary information to ensure you have a smooth and productive experience at our practice.

Salma | Pharmacy Technician: Adnaan

IT Support: Travel or Iqra/Admin Team – Ext 216 (Surgery Connect) - 📱07943760680

Internal Urgent Communication: Use EMIS screen message

Panic Button: On the EMIS homepage, double-click the 🔴red dot to alert all members in the building of an emergency for the following:

Violent or threatening behaviour in reception or waiting areas.

Security breaches or immediate safety risks.

Medical emergencies requiring rapid staff assistance, e.g.:

(Severe chest pain or tightness – suspected heart attack)

(Severe shortness of breath or wheezing – potential asthma attack or anaphylaxis)

(Sudden weakness, facial droop, or slurred speech – possible stroke)

(Seizure or unresponsive patient – possible cardiac arrest or collapse)

(Profuse bleeding or severe allergic reaction – anaphylaxis)

(Signs of sepsis—refer to the sepsis warning signs displayed on the reception boards)

After triggering the alarm, follow safety protocols: secure reception if needed, and immediately alert the duty doctor or Practice Manager.

3. Induction & Orientation for New/Locum Staff

Notify staff of new arrivals, allocate mentor/work area, provide induction pack and organisation chart.

3. Induction & Orientation for New/Locum Staff

Notify staff of new arrivals, allocate mentor/work area, provide induction pack and organisation chart.

Tour: parking, kitchen, toilets, fire exits/extinguishers, visitors book, phones, photocopier, stationery, computer access, mail points.

Explain alarm codes, PPE, infection control, spillage kits, lone worker expectations, zero-tolerance statement.

Clarify job title, responsibilities, line manager, expectations.

Demonstrate EMIS, filing, telephones, leave/sickness reporting, and communication channels.

4. Learning Culture & Incident Reporting

Incident Reporting: Urgent EMIS task to Lead GP and Practice Manager.

Sharing Learning: Monthly meetings, TeamsNet updates, ad hoc emails.

Protected Learning Time: For training, own tasks, SARS and admin duties.

Raising Concerns/Bullying: Report to Practice Manager or Lead GP.

5. Registrations - Refer to the Registration Guide

No ID or proof of address required.

Homeless/boat patients: register under practice address.

Offer paper or online registration—patient’s choice.

Register within 5 working days.

Accept temporary and permanent registrations.

docTitle: The Riverside Practice Reception and Admin Staff Guide

source.path: rag/sources/The Riverside Practice Reception and Admin Staff Guide.docx

text:
patients: register under practice address.

Offer paper or online registration—patient’s choice.

Register within 5 working days.

Accept temporary and permanent registrations.

Out-of-area patients: advise that home visits are not available outside catchment (map on blue board).

6. Booking & Arrivals

Always book/manage appointments same day—never ask to call back.

Confirm and update contact details and preferred communication method.

Offer external services: Pharmacy First, MEC Clinic, NHS 111 (dental only), Enhanced Access clinics (Hackney Downs Hub, Sat all day, 7 am starts, till 8 pm on weekdays).

Document reasons if unable to book.

Check in promptly on EMIS, maintain confidentiality, and never turn away visibly unwell patients—alert duty doctor.

7. Late Arrivals Protocol

Over 10 mins late: Politely explain delay and send urgent EMIS screen message to clinician to check if they can be seen or rebooked.

Emergencies/unwell: escalate to duty doctor—do not turn away.

Record late arrivals in EMIS; follow up persistent lateness.

8. Duty Doctor Calls

Qualifies as duty calls: urgent symptoms, hospitals, coroners, social services, safeguarding, urgent pharmacy queries, or any case needing immediate input.

Keep caller on the line.

docTitle: The Riverside Practice Reception and Admin Staff Guide

source.path: rag/sources/The Riverside Practice Reception and Admin Staff Guide.docx

text:
Doctor Calls

Qualifies as duty calls: urgent symptoms, hospitals, coroners, social services, safeguarding, urgent pharmacy queries, or any case needing immediate input.

Keep caller on the line.

Transfer directly to duty doctor and add to urgent telephone list on EMIS.

Document call and outcome.

9. Triage & Urgent Symptoms

Use triage protocol to assess urgency.

Routine: Book same day when possible.

Non-urgent follow-ups: Book with same GP for continuity by sending them a task; otherwise book in with duty doctor.

Escalate urgent/uncertain cases immediately.

Document conversations and actions in EMIS.

10. Referrals

Routine: Action within 48 hrs, log in ERS, notify patient.

Two-week wait: Action within 24 hrs, book live, if possible, record details, advise follow-up if not contacted, code two-week wait template and create a diary entry of 2 week’s time.

11. Blood Results

Normal: If patient calls, task requesting doctor (or duty doctor if unavailable).

Abnormal or Urgent results: Flag urgently to duty doctor.

Document all actions in EMIS.

12. Prescriptions

Send acute/past drug requests to lead pharmacist.

Calculate repeat due dates and match batch issues to expected end date.

Monitoring Medications – send to pharmacist:

in EMIS.

12. Prescriptions

Send acute/past drug requests to lead pharmacist.

Calculate repeat due dates and match batch issues to expected end date.

Monitoring Medications – send to pharmacist:

Warfarin (request INR & code), Apixaban, Rivaroxaban, Dabigatran, Edoxaban.

Methotrexate, Leflunomide, Sulfasalazine, Hydroxychloroquine, Azathioprine, Mycophenolate, Ciclosporin, Tacrolimus.

Lithium, Amiodarone, High-dose corticosteroids, Clozapine (sec. care), ACE inhibitors/ARBs.

Escalate bleeding, infection, bruising, breathlessness, or severe side effects to duty doctor.

13. Workflow Tasks & Emails

Routine tasks/emails: 48 hrs. Urgent: 24 hrs.

Code emails/documents correctly.

All clinical documents and results → workflow to Dr RG.

Assign tasks appropriately; use protected admin time.

14. Medical Records Requests (SARS)

Log date received and EMIS number on board.

Task Practice Manager and Reception Lead.

Complete within 30 days unless extension agreed.

15. Confidentiality & Secure Communication

Use EMIS numbers instead of names in shared spaces.

Share data only via nhs.net/gov.uk.

For others, gain consent and mark [SECURE] in subject of EMAIL.

Check consent before sending medical info.

Check safeguarding flags before sharing.

16. Infection Control & Health & Safety

IPC Lead: Practice Manager & Practice Nurse.

Follow PPE and bare-below-elbows.

Specimens: Store securely, print label with date of collection, place in red box, remind patients to call for results.

Fridge temps: Log start/end of shifts.

Equipment: Oxygen (Rm 4), Defibrillator (Reception), Spill Kits (Reception), Panic Alarms (EMIS).

Business Community Plan: TeamsNet & Reception Board.

Report hazards; join fire drills.

17. Emergency Preparedness

Collapsed patient/suicidal caller: For Collapsed patient, Press Panic Button and for Duty Doctor Calls, alert duty doctor immediately (urgent EMIS message, call or enter their room). Check board for Duty Doctor Schedule.

Know AED, oxygen, spill kits, fire exits.

Violent incidents: Press Panic Button on EMIS lock reception, await instructions.

18. Locking-Up Procedures (Summary)

Check/close all windows and doors.

18. Locking-Up Procedures (Summary)

Check/close all windows and doors.

Turn off lights, computers, heaters, photocopiers, kitchen appliances (Except Fridge), staffroom equipment.

Switch phones to night service (6:30 pm), lock front/admin doors.

Exit via Front door only and not back door, ensure locked when left.

No lone working with patients—arrange cover or stay.

19. Safeguarding

Leads: Dr Rajiv Goel; backup Dr Rishi Aggarwal or Practice Manager.

External Contacts: blue board.

Receptionists complete Level 2 safeguarding.

Code carers, disabilities, veteran, safeguarding alerts.

Use safeguarding proforma/flowchart for concerns on Blue Board.

20. Equity of Access & Inclusion

Catchment map: blue board.

Out-of-area: advise no home visits.

Record communication needs/formats (Accessible Info Standard).

Offer interpreters/hearing loops/reasonable adjustments - Interview room use for breastfeeding mothers or patients requesting quieter place sensitive to noise and crowded places and use for confidential conversations.

Flag carers and LD/autistic patients.

Be aware of veterans and Safe Surgery schemes.

21. Patient Communication & Complaints

Complaints Lead: Practice Manager.

Targets: Acknowledge 3 days; respond 10 days.

Log all complaints.

Signpost to ICB Complaints if requested.

Share lessons learned at Practice Meetings/TeamsNet.

22. Partnerships & Community Links

Engage PPG for feedback.

Use Better Together Hackney, social prescribers, coffee mornings, walking groups, IT sessions.

Use PCN hubs/ARRS for Enhanced Access bookings.

23. Staff Wellbeing & Performance

Breaks after stressful calls; rotate duties.

Take regular screen breaks

Open-door policy with PM.

Therapy via OT if needed.

Report performance or bullying concerns to PM or Lead GP.

24. Other Key Awareness Areas

Clear desk: secure notes/pads.

Stock: report low levels.

Update boards/Information Folder in Reception: remove outdated info.

Teamwork & self-care: support colleagues, escalate when unsure, take breaks.

25. Whistleblowing & Freedom to Speak Up (Summary)

Guardian: Iqra Khan (Practice Manager).

Escalation: If involves her, go to Dr Rajiv Goel.

25. Whistleblowing & Freedom to Speak Up (Summary)

Guardian: Iqra Khan (Practice Manager).

Escalation: If involves her, go to Dr Rajiv Goel.

Process: Report verbally or in writing; prompt, confidential investigation.

External options: GMC or ICB if unresolved.

Protection: No victimisation for genuine concerns; malicious claims = disciplinary.

Follow NHS Freedom to Speak Up principles—raise issues early.

26. Extra Responsibilities & Reporting

Incident Reporting Folder: Log all significant events, near misses, or safety issues in the Incident Report Folder (kept in Reception). Notify the Practice Manager or Lead GP immediately via EMIS urgent task or screen message.

Duty of Candour Book: Record any incidents where an error or harm has occurred. Inform the Practice Manager or Lead GP so the patient can be informed honestly and promptly.

Risk Assessments:

Moving & Handling: Assess before lifting or moving heavy items—use correct technique or request assistance.

Infection Control: Perform personal risk assessments (e.g. PPE availability, sharps safety, cleaning protocols).

Daily Awareness:

Check notice boards and the EMIS Homepage for new protocols or safety updates on TeamsNet.

docTitle: THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE sharps injury poster

source.path: rag/sources/THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE sharps injury poster.docx

text:
THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE

SHARPS, NEEDLESTICK AND SPLASHING INJURIES

These are incidents involving contamination with blood or body fluids, except urine or faeces unless they are visibly blood stained

ACTION TO BE TAKEN BY AN INJURED MEMBER OF STAFF FOLLOWING AN INCIDENT: FIRST AID AND IMMEDIATE HELP.

Encourage bleeding where the skin is punctured.

Wash thoroughly with soap under running warm water. Do not use a scrubbing brush.

If eyes are involved, wash immediately with water for 5-10 minutes (use tap water or sterile water if available). If the mouth is contaminated rinse with plenty of water.

Where massive contamination of unbroken skin has occurred, remove contaminated clothing and wash all affected areas with copious amounts of water.

Refer initially to one of the doctors in the Practice, and liaise with the Needle stick Nurse - (Tel.07745306654) and/or Occupational Health on 0203 594 6690 or 0203 594 6548 who can advise what further action is necessary.

If unable to contact Occupational Health within 15 minutes of the first attempt, GO TO A&E at HOMERTON HOSPITAL and in out of hours, on weekends and Bank Holidays

If unable to contact Occupational Health within 15 minutes of the first attempt, GO TO A&E at HOMERTON HOSPITAL and in out of hours, on weekends and Bank Holidays

Obtain sufficient information to identify the patient and the member of staff. Take a focused and impartial history to identify risk of HIV, HEP B (HBV) and HEP C (HCV).

If at high risk for HIV start Post Exposure Prophylaxis preferably within an hour, but worthwhile up to 36 hours post-exposure. Find HBV status of “recipient” and consider booster even if good immunity, consider HEP B immunoglobulins.

Note the type of injury, depth, gauge of needle, if used for injecting or aspiration, and if hollow bore or bloodstained. SEE PRACTICE NEEDLESTICK PROTOCOL.

Counsel and consent “donor” to take blood for immediate testing regardless of history.

Counsel and consent “recipient” for bloods to be taken and stored for HIV, HBV and HCV. All samples should be kept for 12 weeks and add needle stick injury on the request from.

Ensure that your manager or immediate senior is informed promptly of the incident. Out of hours inform the practice manager or Dr Goel. The person who has received the injury should complete an incident form, which is kept in reception.

text:
THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE

CHAPERONE POLICY (Updated 2026)

Policy Name: Chaperone PolicyPractice: Riverside PracticeApproved by: Dr R Goel ( GP Partner)Policy Owner: Iqra Khan ( Practice Manager)Review Date: March 2027

1. Introduction

This policy is designed to protect patients and staff during consultations and examinations and to ensure examinations are conducted in a safe, respectful and professional environment.

Riverside Practice recognises that the use of chaperones forms an important part of maintaining sexual safety, professional boundaries and patient dignity.

This policy supports the practice’s:

Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy

NHS Sexual Safety Charter commitments

Chaperoning principles issued by NHS England (December 2025)

Chaperones help safeguard:

patient dignity

professional standards

clinicians and staff from misunderstanding or false allegations.

2. Definition of a Chaperone

A chaperone is a trained member of staff who is present during an examination or procedure to:

support the patient

safeguard the patient

act as an impartial observer

assist the clinician where appropriate

ensure professional standards are maintained.

docTitle: THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE updated Chaperone Policy 2026

source.path: rag/sources/THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE updated Chaperone Policy 2026.docx

text:
during an examination or procedure to:

support the patient

safeguard the patient

act as an impartial observer

assist the clinician where appropriate

ensure professional standards are maintained.

A family member or friend cannot act as a formal chaperone, although they may remain present for reassurance if the patient wishes.

3. When a Chaperone Should Be Offered

A chaperone must be offered for all intimate examinations.

Intimate examinations include but are not limited to:

breast examinations

genital examinations

rectal examinations

pelvic examinations

procedures involving exposure of intimate areas.

The clinician should also consider offering a chaperone when:

the examination may be perceived as intimate

the patient appears uncomfortable

there may be a language barrier

the patient has learning difficulties

there is a history of trauma or vulnerability

cultural sensitivities may apply

the clinician feels a chaperone is appropriate.

Patients have the right to decline a chaperone, but this must be documented.

If the clinician feels uncomfortable proceeding without a chaperone, they may:

explain why a chaperone is recommended

delay the examination

refer the patient to another clinician if appropriate.

docTitle: THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE updated Chaperone Policy 2026

source.path: rag/sources/THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE updated Chaperone Policy 2026.docx

text:
the clinician feels uncomfortable proceeding without a chaperone, they may:

explain why a chaperone is recommended

delay the examination

refer the patient to another clinician if appropriate.

4. Chaperones for Children and Young People

For patients under the age of 18, a formal chaperone must be present for intimate examinations.

A parent, guardian or carer may also be present if the young person wishes, but this does not replace the need for a trained chaperone.

Safeguarding considerations must always be taken into account.

5. Professional Conduct During Examination

Clinicians must:

explain the examination clearly before starting

obtain patient consent

maintain dignity and privacy

allow the patient privacy to undress and dress

use appropriate draping where possible

maintain a professional manner at all times.

Humour or comments that may be misinterpreted should be avoided.

6. Offering a Chaperone

Patients must be made aware that they can request a chaperone.

This may be achieved through:

signage in consulting rooms

patient information materials

verbal explanation by clinicians.

If a patient requests a chaperone:

the examination must not proceed without one

docTitle: THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE updated Chaperone Policy 2026

source.path: rag/sources/THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE updated Chaperone Policy 2026.docx

text:
be achieved through:

signage in consulting rooms

patient information materials

verbal explanation by clinicians.

If a patient requests a chaperone:

the examination must not proceed without one

if none are available, the appointment should be rearranged unless clinically urgent.

7. Who Can Act as a Chaperone

Where possible, chaperones should be clinical staff trained in the role.

Appropriate staff may include:

Practice Nurses

Healthcare Assistants

other trained clinical staff.

Where non-clinical staff act as chaperones:

the patient must agree to this

the staff member must have received appropriate training

they must understand their responsibilities.

All chaperones must be:

trained in the role

aware of professional boundaries

confident in raising concerns if necessary.

8. Role of the Chaperone

The chaperone should:

introduce themselves to the patient

explain their role

remain present throughout the examination

position themselves so they can observe the procedure

respect patient dignity and confidentiality.

The chaperone should not:

participate in conversation unless appropriate

interfere with the consultation unless a concern arises.

docTitle: THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE updated Chaperone Policy 2026

source.path: rag/sources/THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE updated Chaperone Policy 2026.docx

text:
the procedure

respect patient dignity and confidentiality.

The chaperone should not:

participate in conversation unless appropriate

interfere with the consultation unless a concern arises.

If the chaperone witnesses inappropriate behaviour or has concerns, they must raise this immediately with the Practice Manager or Lead GP in line with the Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy.

9. Documentation

The clinician must record in the patient record:

that a chaperone was offered

whether the patient accepted or declined

the name and role of the chaperone

if declined, that the patient declined.

If the patient declines a chaperone but the clinician proceeds with the examination, the notes should reflect this clearly.

If the patient requests a chaperone and none is available, the consultation should be rescheduled unless clinically urgent.

Chaperones should record any concerns or incidents that occur during the examination.

10. Confidentiality

The chaperone must respect the patient’s right to confidentiality.

The chaperone should only be present during the examination and leave once the examination is complete unless otherwise requested.

All staff must follow practice confidentiality policies.

11. Home Visits

docTitle: THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE updated Chaperone Policy 2026

source.path: rag/sources/THE RIVERSIDE PRACTICE updated Chaperone Policy 2026.docx

text:
should only be present during the examination and leave once the examination is complete unless otherwise requested.

All staff must follow practice confidentiality policies.

11. Home Visits

Occasionally, intimate examinations may be required during home visits.

Where possible:

a chaperone should be arranged in advance

if not possible, the clinician should assess risk carefully

documentation must clearly record the circumstances.

If the clinician feels a chaperone is necessary but one cannot be arranged, the examination should be postponed unless clinically urgent.

12. Concerns or Allegations

If a patient or staff member raises a concern about conduct during an examination, this must be reported immediately to:

Practice Manager

Lead GP

Freedom to Speak Up Guardian

The matter will be handled in line with the practice’s Sexual Misconduct and Sexual Safety Policy, and may involve escalation to:

NHS England

safeguarding authorities

professional regulators

the police where appropriate.

13. Training

All staff acting as chaperones must receive training covering:

the role and responsibilities of a chaperone

patient dignity

safeguarding awareness

professional boundaries

responding to concerns.
